{{short description|Tendency to act on a whim without considering consequences}}
[[File:MRI of orbitofrontal cortex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[Orbitofrontal cortex]], part of the prefrontal cortex that shapes decision-making]]
In [[psychology]], '''impulsivity''' (or '''impulsiveness''') is a tendency to act on a [[Impulse (psychology)|whim]], displaying behavior characterized by little or no [[forethought]], reflection, or consideration of the consequences.<ref>{{cite book |last=VandenBos |first=G. R. |year=2007 |pages=470 |title=APA dictionary of psychology |url= https://archive.org/details/apadictionaryofp00vand |url-access=registration |location=Washington, DC |publisher=APA|isbn=9781591473800 }}</ref> Impulsive actions are typically "poorly conceived, prematurely expressed, unduly risky, or inappropriate to the situation that often result in undesirable consequences,"<ref name = "Daruna">{{cite book |last1=Daruna |first1=J. H. |last2=Barnes |first2=P. A. |year=1993 |chapter=A neurodevelopmental view of impulsivity |pages=[https://archive.org/details/impulsiveclientt0000unse/page/n23 23]–37 |doi=10.1037/10500-002 |editor1-first=William George |editor1-last=McCown |editor2-first=Judith L. |editor2-last=Johnson |editor3-first=Myrna B. |editor3-last=Shure |title=The Impulsive Client: Theory, Research, and Treatment |url=https://archive.org/details/impulsiveclientt0000unse |location= Washington, DC |publisher=American Psychological Association |isbn=978-1-55798-208-7}}</ref> which imperil long-term [[goal]]s and strategies for success.<ref name = "M&G10">{{cite book |last1=Madden |first1=Gregory J. |last2=Johnson |first2=Patrick S. |year=2010 |chapter=A Delay-Discounting Primer |editor1-first=Gregory Jude |editor1-last=Madden |editor2-first=Warren K. |editor2-last=Bickel |title=Impulsivity: The Behavioral and Neurological Science of Discounting |pages=11–37 |location= Washington, DC |publisher=American Psychological Association |isbn=978-1-4338-0477-9}}</ref> Impulsivity can be classified as a multifactorial [[Construct (philosophy of science)|construct]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/PL00005481 |title=Varieties of impulsivity |year=1999 |last1=Evenden |first1=J. L. |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume= 146 |issue=4 |pages=348–61 |pmid=10550486 |s2cid=5972342 }}</ref> A functional variety of impulsivity has also been suggested, which involves action without much forethought in appropriate situations that can and does result in desirable consequences. "When such actions have positive outcomes, they tend not to be seen as signs of impulsivity, but as indicators of [[boldness]], [[wiktionary:fast|quickness]], spontaneity, [[courageousness]], or unconventionality."<ref name="Daruna" /><ref name= "Dickman" /> Thus, the construct of impulsivity includes at least two independent components: first, acting without an appropriate amount of deliberation,<ref name= "Daruna"/> which may or may not be functional; and second, [[deferred gratification|choosing short-term gains over long-term ones]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Rachlin |first1=Howard |year=2000 |chapter=Self-Control as an Abstraction of Environmental Feedback |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KWp-EJaEAUIC&pg=PA183 |page=183 |title=The Science of Self-Control |location=Cambridge, MA |publisher=Harvard University Press |isbn=978-0-674-00093-3}}</ref>

Impulsivity is both a [[Facet (psychology)|facet]] of personality and a major component of various disorders, including [[FASD]], [[autism]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=G Aman |first1=Michael |last2=A Farmer |first2=Christan |title=Treatment of Inattention, Overactivity, and Impulsiveness in Autism Spectrum Disorders |journal=Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Clinics of North America |date=2008 |volume=17 |issue=4 |pages=713–vii |doi=10.1016/j.chc.2008.06.009 |pmid=18775366 |pmc=3805750 }}</ref> [[ADHD]],<ref>{{cite journal |doi= 10.1037/0033-2909.127.5.571 |title=Is ADHD a disinhibitory disorder? |year=2001 |last1=Nigg |first1=Joel T. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=127 |issue=5 |pages= 571–98 |pmid=11548968}}</ref> [[substance use disorder]]s,<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/00132576-200302020-00001 |title=Relationships Among Laboratory and Psychometric Measures of Impulsivity: Implications in Substance Abuse and Dependence |year=2003 |last1=Lane |first1=Scott D. |last2=Cherek |first2=Don R. |last3=Rhoades |first3= Howard M. |last4=Pietras |first4=Cynthia J. |last5=Tcheremissine |first5=Oleg V. |journal=Addictive Disorders & Their Treatment |volume=2 |issue=2 |pages=33–40|s2cid= 143129215 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/1064-1297.5.3.256 |title=Impulsive and self-control choices in opioid-dependent patients and non-drug-using control patients: Drug and monetary rewards |year=1997 |last1=Madden |first1=G.J. |last2=Petry |first2=N.M. |last3=Badger |first3=G.J. | last4=Bickel |first4= W. K. |journal= [[Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology]] |volume=5 |issue=3 |pages=256–62 |pmid=9260073}}</ref> [[bipolar disorder]],<ref name="Henry">{{cite journal |doi= 10.1016/S0022-3956(01)00038-3 |title=Affective instability and impulsivity in borderline personality and bipolar II disorders: Similarities and differences |year=2001 |last1= Henry |first1=Chantal |last2=Mitropoulou |first2=Vivian |last3=New |first3=Antonia S |last4=Koenigsberg |first4=Harold W |last5=Silverman |first5=Jeremy |last6= Siever |first6= Larry J |journal=Journal of Psychiatric Research |volume=35 |issue=6 |pages=307–12 |pmid=11684137}}</ref> [[antisocial personality disorder]],<ref name= "Horn">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0028-3932(03)00077-0 |title=Response inhibition and impulsivity: An fMRI study |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_neuropsychologia_2003_41_14/page/1959 |year=2003 |last1=Horn |first1=N.R. |last2=Dolan |first2=M. |last3=Elliott |first3=R. |last4=Deakin |first4=J.F.W. |last5=Woodruff |first5=P.W.R. |journal=Neuropsychologia |volume=41 |issue=14 |pages=1959–66 |pmid=14572528 |s2cid=31957781 }}</ref> and [[borderline personality disorder]].<ref name="Henry"/> Abnormal patterns of impulsivity have also been noted in instances of [[acquired brain injury]]<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/bin.173 |title=Impulsivity, self-control, and delay discounting in persons with acquired brain injury |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_behavioral-interventions_2005-02_20_1/page/101 |year=2005 |last1=Dixon |first1=Mark R. |last2=Jacobs |first2=Eric A. |last3=Sanders |first3=Scott |last4=Guercio |first4=John M. |last5=Soldner |first5=James |last6= Parker-Singler |first6=Susan |last7=Robinson |first7=Ashton |last8=Small |first8=Stacey |last9=Dillen |first9=Jeffrey E. |journal=Behavioral Interventions |volume=20 |issue= 1 |pages=101–20  }}</ref> and [[neurodegenerative diseases]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nrneurol.2010.148 |title=Decision-making cognition in neurodegenerative diseases |year=2010 |last1=Gleichgerrcht |first1=Ezequiel |last2=Ibáñez |first2=Agustín |last3=Roca |first3=María |last4=Torralva |first4=Teresa |last5=Manes |first5=Facundo |journal=Nature Reviews Neurology |volume=6 |issue=11 |pages=611–23 |pmid=21045795 |s2cid=13333018 }}</ref> [[#Neurobiological findings|Neurobiological findings]] suggest that there are specific brain regions involved in impulsive behavior,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Corsini |first1=Raymond Joseph |year=1999 |title=The Dictionary of Psychology |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=1-58391-028-X |page=476}}</ref><ref name="Berlin" /><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1089/neu.2005.22.613 |title=Deficits in Decision-Making in Head Injury Survivors |year=2005 |last1=Salmond |first1=C.H. |last2=Menon |first2=D.K. |last3=Chatfield |first3=D.A. |last4=Pickard |first4=J.D. |last5=Sahakian |first5=B.J. |journal=Journal of Neurotrauma |volume=22 |issue=6 |pages=613–22 |pmid=15941371}}</ref> although different brain networks may contribute to different manifestations of impulsivity,<ref name="Delay Discounting in the Brain">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hampton WH, Venkatraman V, Olson IR | title = Dissociable frontostriatal white matter connectivity underlies reward and motor impulsivity | language = en | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 150 | issue = 4 | pages = 336–343 | year = 2017 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2017.02.021 | pmid = 28189592 | pmc = 5494201 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nn.3092 |title=Adolescent impulsivity phenotypes characterized by distinct brain networks |year=2012 |last1=Whelan |first1=Robert |last2=Conrod |first2=Patricia J |last3=Poline |first3=Jean-Baptiste |last4=Lourdusamy |first4=Anbarasu |last5=Banaschewski |first5=Tobias |last6=Barker |first6=Gareth J |last7=Bellgrove |first7=Mark A |last8=Büchel |first8= Christian |last9=Byrne |first9=Mark |last10=Cummins |first10=Tarrant D R |last11=Fauth-Bühler |first11=Mira |last12=Flor |first12=Herta |last13=Gallinat |first13=Jürgen |last14=Heinz |first14=Andreas |last15=Ittermann |first15=Bernd |last16=Mann |first16=Karl |last17=Martinot |first17=Jean-Luc |last18=Lalor |first18=Edmund C |last19= Lathrop |first19=Mark |last20=Loth |first20=Eva |last21=Nees |first21=Frauke |last22=Paus |first22=Tomas |last23=Rietschel |first23=Marcella |last24=Smolka |first24= Michael N |last25=Spanagel |first25=Rainer |last26=Stephens |first26=David N |last27=Struve |first27=Maren |last28=Thyreau |first28=Benjamin |last29=Vollstaedt-Klein |first29=Sabine |last30=Robbins |first30=Trevor W |journal=Nature Neuroscience |volume=15 |issue=6 |pages=920–5 |pmid=22544311|s2cid=18808674 |display-authors=8 }}</ref> and that [[#Genetics|genetics]] may play a role.<ref name=":0" />

Many actions contain both impulsive and compulsive features, but impulsivity and compulsivity are functionally distinct. Impulsivity and compulsivity are interrelated in that each exhibits a tendency to act prematurely or without considered thought and often include negative outcomes.<ref name="Berlin, H. A. 2008">{{cite journal |first1=Heather A. |last1=Berlin |first2=Eric |last2=Hollander |date=July 1, 2008 |title=Understanding the Differences Between Impulsivity and Compulsivity |issue=8 |journal=Psychiatric Times |series=Psychiatric Times Vol 25 No 8 |volume=25 |url=http://www.psychiatrictimes.com/understanding-differences-between-impulsivity-and-compulsivity/page/0/1 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203001449/http://www.psychiatrictimes.com/understanding-differences-between-impulsivity-and-compulsivity/page/0/1 |archive-date=December 3, 2013 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Oldham |first1=J.M. |last2=Hollander |first2=E.|author3-link=Andrew E. Skodol |last3=Skodol |first3=A.E. |year=1996 |title=Impulsivity and Compulsivity |location=Washington D.C. |publisher=American Psychiatric Press}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref>  Compulsivity may be on a continuum with compulsivity on one end and impulsivity on the other, but research has been contradictory on this point.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/eat.20169 |title=Impulsivity and compulsivity in bulimia nervosa |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_international-journal-of-eating-disorders_2005-11_38_3/page/244 |year=2005 |last1=Engel |first1=Scott G. |last2=Corneliussen |first2=Stephanie J. |last3=Wonderlich |first3=Stephen A. |last4=Crosby |first4=Ross D. |last5=Le Grange |first5=Daniel |last6=Crow |first6=Scott |last7=Klein |first7=Marjorie |last8=Bardone-Cone |first8=Anna |last9=Peterson |first9=Carol |last10=Joiner |first10=Thomas |last11=Mitchell |first11=James E. |last12=Steiger |first12=Howard |journal=International Journal of Eating Disorders |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=244–51 |pmid=16211626|display-authors=8 }}</ref> Compulsivity occurs in response to a perceived risk or threat, impulsivity occurs in response to a perceived immediate gain or benefit,<ref name="Berlin, H. A. 2008"/> and, whereas compulsivity involves repetitive actions, impulsivity involves unplanned reactions.

Impulsivity is a common feature of the conditions of [[gambling]] and [[alcohol addiction]]. Research has shown that individuals with either of these addictions discount delayed money (reduce its subjective value to them) at higher rates than those without, and that the presence of gambling and alcohol abuse lead to additive effects on discounting.<ref name="Andrade Alessi Petry 2013 pp. 241–248">{{cite journal | last1=Andrade | first1=Leonardo F. | last2=Alessi | first2=Sheila M. | last3=Petry | first3=Nancy M. | title=The Effects of Alcohol Problems and Smoking on Delay Discounting in Individuals with Gambling Problems | journal=Journal of Psychoactive Drugs | publisher=Informa UK Limited | volume=45 | issue=3 | year=2013 | issn=0279-1072 | doi=10.1080/02791072.2013.803645 | pages=241–248| pmc=3816387 | pmid=24175489}}</ref>

==Impulse==
An '''impulse''' is a wish or urge, particularly a sudden one. It can be considered as a normal and fundamental part of [[human]] [[thought]] processes, but also one that can become problematic, as in a condition like [[obsessive-compulsive disorder]],<ref>{{cite web|title=Acting on Impulse|url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/short-history-mental-health/201210/acting-impulse|publisher=Psychology Today|access-date=20 February 2016}}</ref>{{ums|date=November 2017}} [[borderline personality disorder]], [[autism spectrum disorder]], [[attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]], or in [[fetal alcohol spectrum disorder]]s.

The ability to control impulses, or more specifically control the desire to act on them, is an important factor in [[Personality psychology|personality]] and [[socialization]]. [[Delayed gratification|Deferred gratification]], also known as [[Impulse control disorder|impulse control]] is an example of this, concerning impulses primarily relating to things that a person wants or desires. Delayed gratification comes when one avoids acting on initial impulses. Delayed gratification has been studied in relation to childhood obesity. Resisting the urge to act on impulses is important to teach children, because it teaches the value of delayed gratification.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Caleza|first1=Carolina|last2=Yañez-Vico|first2=Rosa M.|last3=Mendoza|first3=Asunción|last4=Iglesias-Linares|first4=Alejandro|date=2016-02-01|title=Childhood Obesity and Delayed Gratification Behavior: A Systematic Review of Experimental Studies|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022347615011671|journal=The Journal of Pediatrics|language=en|volume=169|pages=201–207.e1|doi=10.1016/j.jpeds.2015.10.008|pmid=26563536|issn=0022-3476|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
{{blockquote|Many psychological problems are characterized by a loss of control or a lack of control in specific situations. Usually, this lack of control is part of a pattern of behavior that also involves other [[maladaptive|maladaptive thoughts and actions]], such as [[substance abuse]] problems or sexual disorders like the [[paraphilia]]s (e.g. [[pedophilia]] and [[exhibitionism]]). When loss of control is only a component of a disorder, it usually does not have to be a part of the behavior pattern, and other symptoms must also be present for the diagnosis to be made. (Franklin<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.psychologyinfo.com/problems/impulse_control.html |title=Psychological Impulse Control Disorders |access-date=2022-09-07 |archive-date=2018-01-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180119202343/http://psychologyinfo.com/problems/impulse_control.html |url-status=dead }}</ref>{{ums|date=November 2017}})}}

== The five traits that can lead to impulsive actions ==
For many years it was understood that impulsivity was itself a trait, but with further analysis it has been found that there are five distinct traits that can lead to impulsive actions:
positive urgency, 
negative urgency,
[[sensation seeking]], 
lack of planning, and
[[Psychological resilience|lack of perseverance]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Gregory T. |last2=Cyders |first2=Melissa A. |title=Integrating affect and impulsivity: The role of positive and negative urgency in substance use risk |journal=Drug and Alcohol Dependence |date=June 2016 |volume=163 |issue=Suppl 1 |pages=S3–S12 |doi=10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2015.08.038 |pmid=27306729 |pmc=4911536 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fischer |first1=Sarah |last2=Smith |first2=Gregory T. |last3=Anderson |first3=Kristen G. |title=Clarifying the role of impulsivity in bulimia nervosa |journal=International Journal of Eating Disorders |date=May 2003 |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=406–411 |doi=10.1002/eat.10165 |pmid=12658670 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Gregory T. |last2=Guller |first2=Leila |last3=Zapolski |first3=Tamika C. B. |title=A Comparison of Two Models of Urgency: Urgency Predicts Both Rash Action and Depression in Youth |journal=Clinical Psychological Science |date=July 2013 |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=266–275 |doi=10.1177/2167702612470647 |pmid=25419495 |pmc=4238932 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dvorak |first1=Robert D. |last2=Pearson |first2=Matthew R. |last3=Kuvaas |first3=Nicholas J. |title=The Five-Factor Model of Impulsivity-Like Traits and Emotional Lability in Aggressive Behavior: Impulsivity, Emotional Lability, and Aggression |journal=Aggressive Behavior |date=May 2013 |volume=39 |issue=3 |pages=222–228 |doi=10.1002/ab.21474 |pmid=23471690 |pmc=4625555 }}</ref>

== Associated behavioral and societal problems ==

=== Attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder ===
[[Attention deficit-hyperactivity disorder]] (ADHD) is a multiple component disorder involving [[inattention]], impulsivity, and [[hyperactivity]].  The [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders]] (DSM-IV-TR)<ref name="DSM IV TR">{{cite book |publisher=American Psychiatric Association |year=2000 |title=Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders |volume=1 |edition=4th |doi=10.1176/appi.books.9780890423349 |doi-broken-date=12 July 2025 |isbn=978-0-89042-334-9}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> breaks ADHD into three subtypes according to the behavioral symptoms:
Predominantly Inattentive Type,
Predominantly Hyperactive-Impulsive Type, and
Combined Type.

Predominantly hyperactive-impulsive type symptoms may include
fidgeting and squirming in seats,
talking nonstop,
dashing around and touching or playing with anything in sight,
having trouble sitting still during dinner/school/story time,
being constantly in motion, and
having difficulty doing quiet tasks or activities.

Other manifestations primarily of impulsivity include
being very impatient, 
having difficulty waiting for things they want or waiting their turns in games, 
often interrupting conversations or others' activities, or
blurting out inappropriate comments, showing their emotions without restraint, and act without regard for consequences.

Prevalence of the disorder worldwide is estimated to be between 4% and 10%, with reports as low as 2.2% and as high as 17.8%. Variation in rate of diagnoses may be attributed to differences between populations (i.e. culture), and differences in diagnostic methodologies.<ref name="Skounti">{{cite journal |last1=Skounti |first1=Maria |last2=Philalithis |first2=Anastas |last3=Galanakis |first3=Emmanouil |title=Variations in prevalence of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder worldwide |journal=European Journal of Pediatrics |date=21 December 2006 |volume=166 |issue=2 |pages=117–123 |doi=10.1007/s00431-006-0299-5 |pmid=17033803 |s2cid=20342282 }}</ref> Prevalence of ADHD among females is less than half that of males, and females more commonly fall into the inattentive subtype.<ref name="Froehlich">{{cite journal |doi=10.1001/archpedi.161.9.857 |title=Prevalence, Recognition, and Treatment of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder in a National Sample of US Children |year=2007 |last1=Froehlich |first1=Tanya E. |journal=Archives of Pediatrics & Adolescent Medicine |volume=161 |issue=9 |pages=857–64 |pmid=17768285 |last2=Lanphear |first2=BP |last3=Epstein |first3=JN |last4=Barbaresi |first4=WJ |last5=Katusic |first5=SK |last6=Kahn |first6=RS|author1-link=Tanya Froehlich |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Despite an upward trend in diagnoses of the inattentive subtype of ADHD, impulsivity is commonly considered to be the central feature of ADHD, and the impulsive and combined subtypes are the major contributors to the societal costs associated with ADHD.<ref name="Froehlich" /><ref name="Solanto">{{cite journal |doi=10.1023/A:1010329714819 |year=2001 |last1=Solanto |first1=Mary V. |last2=Abikoff |first2=Howard |last3=Sonuga-Barke |first3=Edmund |last4=Schachar |first4=Russell |last5=Logan |first5=Gordon D. |last6=Wigal |first6=Tim |last7=Hechtman |first7=Lily |last8=Hinshaw |first8=Stephen |last9=Turkel |first9=Elihu |journal=Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology |volume=29 |issue=3 |pages=215–28 |pmid=11411784 |title=The ecological validity of delay aversion and response inhibition as measures of impulsivity in AD/HD: A supplement to the NIMH multimodal treatment study of AD/HD  |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-abnormal-child-psychology_2001-06_29_3/page/215 |s2cid=23953111 }}</ref> The estimated cost of illness for a child with ADHD is $14,576 (in 2005 dollars) annually.<ref name="Pelham">{{cite journal |doi=10.1093/jpepsy/jsm022 |title=The Economic Impact of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder in Children and Adolescents |year=2007 |last1=Pelham |first1=William E. |last2=Foster |first2=E. Michael |last3=Robb |first3=Jessica A. |journal=Journal of Pediatric Psychology |volume=32 |issue=6 |pages=711–27 |pmid=17556402|doi-access=free }}</ref> Prevalence of ADHD among prison populations is significantly higher than that of the normal population.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00406-004-0516-z |title=Prevalence of attention deficit–/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and comorbid disorders in young male prison inmates |year=2004 |last1=Rösler |first1=M. |last2=Retz |first2=W. |last3=Retz-Junginger |first3=P. |last4=Hengesch |first4=G. |last5=Schneider |first5=M. |last6=Supprian |first6=T. |last7=Schwitzgebel |first7=P. |last8=Pinhard |first8=K. |last9=Dovi–Akue |first9=N. |last10=Wender |first10=P. |last11=Thome |first11=J. |journal=European Archives of Psychiatry and Clinical Neuroscience |volume=254 |issue=6 |pages=365–71 |pmid=15538605 |s2cid=8737929 |display-authors=8 }}</ref>
	
In both adults<ref name="Kessler">{{cite journal |doi=10.1176/appi.ajp.163.4.716 |title=The Prevalence and Correlates of Adult ADHD in the United States: Results from the National Comorbidity Survey Replication |year=2006 |last1=Kessler |first1=R. C. |last2=Adler |first2=L. |last3=Barkley |first3=R. |last4=Biederman |first4=J. |last5=Conners |first5=C. K. |last6=Demler |first6=O. |last7=Faraone |first7=S. V. |last8=Greenhill |first8=L. L. |last9=Howes |first9=M. J. |last10=Secnik |first10=K |last11=Spencer |first11=T |last12=Ustun |first12=T. B. |last13=Walters |first13=E. E. |last14=Zaslavsky |first14=A. M. |journal=American Journal of Psychiatry |volume=163 |issue=4 |pages=716–23 |pmid=16585449 |pmc=2859678|display-authors=8 }}</ref> and children,<ref name="Wilens2002">{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/00004583-200203000-00005 |title=Psychiatric Comorbidity and Functioning in Clinically Referred Preschool Children and School-Age Youths with ADHD |year=2002 |last1=Wilens |first1=Timothy E. |last2=Biederman |first2=Joseph |last3=Brown |first3=Sarah |last4=Tanguay |first4=Sarah |last5=Monuteaux |first5=Michael C. |last6=Blake |first6=Christie |last7=Spencer |first7=Thomas J. |journal=Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry |volume=41 |issue=3 |pages=262–8 |pmid=11886020}}</ref><ref name="Scahill">{{cite journal |pmid=10944656 |year=2000 |last1=Scahill |first1=L |last2=Schwab-Stone |first2=M |title=Epidemiology of ADHD in school-age children |volume=9 |issue=3 |pages=541–55, vii |journal=Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Clinics of North America|doi=10.1016/S1056-4993(18)30106-8 }}</ref> ADHD has a high rate of comorbidity with other mental health disorders such as [[learning disability]], [[conduct disorder]], [[anxiety disorder]], [[major depressive disorder]], [[bipolar disorder]], and [[substance abuse|substance use]] disorders.

The precise genetic and environmental factors contributing to ADHD are relatively unknown, but [[endophenotype]]s offer a potential middle ground between genes and symptoms.<ref name="Aron">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.biopsych.2004.10.026 |title=The Cognitive Neuroscience of Response Inhibition: Relevance for Genetic Research in Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder |year=2005 |last1=Aron |first1=Adam R. |last2=Poldrack |first2=Russell A. |journal=Biological Psychiatry |volume=57 |issue=11 |pages=1285–92 |pmid=15950000 |s2cid=41786909 }}</ref> ADHD is commonly linked to "core" deficits involving "[[executive function]]," "[[Hyperbolic discounting|delay aversion]]," or "activation/arousal" theories that attempt to explain ADHD through its symptomology.<ref name="Aron" /> Endophenotypes, on the other hand, purport to identify potential behavioral markers that correlate with specific genetic etiology. There is some evidence to support deficits in response inhibition as one such marker.  Problems inhibiting prepotent responses are linked with deficits in pre-frontal cortex (PFC) functioning, which is a common dysfunction associated with ADHD and other impulse-control disorders.<ref name="Ridderinkhof">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.bandc.2004.09.016 |title=Neurocognitive mechanisms of cognitive control: The role of prefrontal cortex in action selection, response inhibition, performance monitoring, and reward-based learning |year=2004 |last1=Ridderinkhof |first1=K. Richard |last2=Van Den Wildenberg |first2=Wery P.M. |last3=Segalowitz |first3=Sidney J. |last4=Carter |first4=Cameron S. |journal=Brain and Cognition |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=129–40 |pmid=15518930 |s2cid=16820592 }}</ref><ref name="Casey">{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/00004583-199703000-00016 |title=Implication of Right Frontostriatal Circuitry in Response Inhibition and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder |year=1997 |last1=Casey |first1=B.J. |last2=Castellanos |first2=F. Xavier |last3=Giedd |first3=Jay N. |last4=Marsh |first4=Wendy L. |last5=Hamburger |first5=Susan D. |last6=Schubert |first6=Anne B. |last7=Vauss |first7=Yolanda C. |last8=Vaituzis |first8=A. Catherine |last9=Dickstein |first9=Daniel P. |last10=Sarfatti |first10=Stacey E. |last11=Rapoport |first11=Judith L. |journal=Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=374–83 |pmid=9055518|display-authors=8 |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1234800 }}</ref>

Evidence-based psychopharmacological and behavioral [[#Treatment of specific disorders of impulsivity|interventions]] exist for ADHD.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=DeVries|first=Melissa|date=2010-12-09|title=Book Review: Pliszka, S. R., UTHSCSA Treating ADHD and Comorbid Disorders: Psychosocial and Psychopharmacological Interventions. New York: Guilford, 2008|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1177/1087054710362216|journal=Journal of Attention Disorders|volume=15|issue=1|pages=92–93|doi=10.1177/1087054710362216|s2cid=143753779|issn=1087-0547|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

=== Substance abuse ===

Impulsivity appears to be linked to all stages of  [[substance abuse]].<ref name="Perry">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00213-008-1173-0 |title=The role of impulsive behavior in drug abuse |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-clinical-psychopharmacology_2008-02_28_1/page/1 |year=2008 |last1=Perry |first1=Jennifer L. |last2=Carroll |first2=Marilyn E. |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=200 |pages=1–26 |pmid=18600315 |issue=1 |s2cid=24587409 }}</ref><ref name="de Wit 2009">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1369-1600.2008.00129.x |title=Impulsivity as a determinant and consequence of drug use: A review of underlying processes |year=2009 |last1=De Wit |first1=Harriet |journal=Addiction Biology |volume=14 |pages=22–31 |pmid=18855805 |issue=1 |pmc=3640851}}</ref>

The acquisition phase of substance abuse involves the escalation from single use to regular use.<ref name="Perry" />  Impulsivity may be related to the acquisition of substance abuse because  of the potential role that instant gratification provided by the substance may offset the larger future benefits of abstaining from the substance, and because people with impaired inhibitory control may not be able to overcome motivating environmental cues, such as [[peer pressure]].<ref name="de Wit 2004">{{cite journal |pmid=15160637 |year=2004 |last1=De Wit |first1=H |last2=Richards |first2=JB |title=Dual determinants of drug use in humans: Reward and impulsivity |volume=50 |pages=19–55 |journal=Nebraska Symposium on Motivation}}</ref>  "Similarly, individuals that discount the value of delayed reinforcers begin to abuse alcohol, marijuana, and cigarettes early in life, while also abusing a wider array of illicit drugs compared to those who discounted delayed reinforcers less."<ref name="Kollins">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0306-4603(02)00220-4 |title=Delay discounting is associated with substance use in college students |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_addictive-behaviors_2003-08_28_6/page/1167 |year=2003 |last1=Kollins |first1=Scott H |journal=Addictive Behaviors |volume=28 |issue=6 |pages=1167–73 |pmid=12834659}}</ref>

Escalation or dysregulation is the next and more severe phase of substance abuse.  In this phase individuals "lose control" of their addiction with large levels of drug consumption and binge drug use.  Animal studies  suggest that individuals with higher levels of impulsivity may be more prone to the escalation stage of substance abuse.<ref name="Perry" />

Impulsivity is also related to the abstinence, relapse, and treatment stages of substance abuse.  People who scored high on the [[Barratt Impulsivity Scale]] (BIS) were more likely to stop treatment for cocaine abuse.<ref name="Moeller">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0740-5472(01)00202-1 |title=The impact of impulsivity on cocaine use and retention in treatment |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-substance-abuse-treatment_2001-12_21_4/page/193 |year=2001 |last1=Moeller |first1=F.Gerard |last2=Dougherty |first2=Donald M. |last3=Barratt |first3=Ernest S. |last4=Schmitz |first4=Joy M. |last5=Swann |first5=Alan C. |last6=Grabowski |first6=John |journal=Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=193–8 |pmid=11777668}}</ref>  Additionally, they adhered to treatment for a shorter duration than people that scored low on impulsivity.<ref name="Moeller"/>  Also, impulsive people had greater cravings for drugs during withdrawal periods and were more likely to relapse.  This effect was shown in a study where smokers that test high on the BIS had increased craving in response to smoking cues, and gave into the cravings more quickly than less impulsive smokers.<ref name="Doran">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00213-007-0832-x |title=Effect of impulsivity on craving and behavioral reactivity to smoking cues |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychopharmacology_2007-10_194_2/page/279 |year=2007 |last1=Doran |first1=Neal |last2=Spring |first2=Bonnie |last3=McChargue |first3=Dennis |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=194 |issue=2 |pages=279–88 |pmid=17594080 |s2cid=35948752 }}</ref>  Taken as a whole the current research suggests that impulsive individuals are less likely to abstain from drugs and more likely to relapse earlier than less impulsive individuals.<ref name="Perry" />

While it is important to note the effect of impulsivity on substance abuse, the reciprocating effect whereby substance abuse can increase impulsivity has also been researched and documented.<ref name="Perry" />   The promoting effect of impulsivity on substance abuse and the effect of substance abuse on increased impulsivity creates a [[positive feedback]] loop that maintains substance seeking behaviors.  It also makes conclusions about the direction of causality difficult.  This phenomenon has been shown to be related to several substances, but not all.  For example, alcohol has been shown to increase impulsivity while amphetamines have had mixed results.<ref name="Perry" />

Substance use disorder [[#Treatment of specific disorders of impulsivity|treatments]] include prescription of medications such as [[acamprosate]], [[buprenorphine]], [[disulfiram]], [[LAAM]], [[methadone]], and [[naltrexone]],<ref name=" McGovern Carroll 2003"/> as well as effective psychotherapeutic treatment like 
[[Integrative behavioral couples therapy|behavioral couples therapy]], [[Cognitive behavioral therapy|CBT]], [[contingency management]], [[motivational interviewing|motivational enhancement therapy]], and [[relapse prevention]].<ref name=" McGovern Carroll 2003"/>

=== Eating ===

Impulsive [[overeating]] spans from an episode of indulgence by an otherwise healthy person to chronic binges by a person with an eating disorder.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

Consumption of a tempting food by non-clinical individuals increases when self-regulatory resources are previously depleted by another task, suggesting that it is caused by a breakdown in [[self control]].<ref name="Vohs">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00250 |title=Self-Regulatory Failure: A Resource-Depletion Approach |year=2000 |last1=Vohs |first1=K. D. |last2=Heatherton |first2=T. F. |journal=Psychological Science |volume=11 |issue=3 |pages=249–54 |pmid=11273412|s2cid=18307595 }}</ref>  Impulsive eating of unhealthy snack foods appears to be regulated by individual differences in impulsivity when self-control is weak and by attitudes towards the snack and towards healthy eating when self-control is strong.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.appet.2011.11.019 |title=Reflective and impulsive influences on unhealthy snacking. The moderating effects of food related self-control |year=2012 |last1=Honkanen |first1=Pirjo |last2=Olsen |first2=Svein Ottar |last3=Verplanken |first3=Bas |last4=Tuu |first4=Ho Huy |journal=Appetite |volume=58 |issue=2 |pages=616–22 |pmid=22138115 |s2cid=39135358 }}</ref> There is also evidence that greater food consumption occurs when people are in a sad mood, although it is possible that this is due more to emotional regulation than to a lack of self-control.<ref name="Baumeister">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.80.1.53 |title=Emotional distress regulation takes precedence over impulse control: If you feel bad, do it! |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_2001-01_80_1/page/53 |year=2001 |last1=Tice |first1=Dianne M. |last2=Bratslavsky |first2=Ellen |last3=Baumeister |first3=Roy F. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=80 |pages=53–67 |pmid=11195891 |issue=1 |s2cid=10596913 }}</ref> In these cases, overeating will only take place if the food is palatable to the person, and if so individual differences in impulsivity can predict the amount of consumption.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2008.09.013 |title=Three ways to resist temptation: The independent contributions of executive attention, inhibitory control, and affect regulation to the impulse control of eating behavior |year=2009 |last1=Hofmann |first1=Wilhelm |last2=Friese |first2=Malte |last3=Roefs |first3=Anne |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |volume=45 |issue=2 |pages=431–5|s2cid=51986961 |url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00686667/document }}</ref>

Chronic overeating is a behavioral component of [[binge eating disorder]], [[compulsive overeating]], and [[bulimia nervosa]]. These diseases are more common for women and may involve eating thousands of calories at a time.  Depending on which of these disorders is the underlying cause, an episode of overeating can have a variety of different motivations. Characteristics common among these three disorders include low [[self-esteem]], [[Depression (mood)|depression]], eating when not physically hungry, preoccupation with food, eating alone due to embarrassment, and feelings of regret or disgust after an episode.  In these cases, overeating is not limited to palatable foods.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.110.1.86 |title=Binge eating as escape from self-awareness |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-bulletin_1991-07_110_1/page/86 |year=1991 |last1=Heatherton |first1=Todd F. |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=110 |pages=86–108 |pmid=1891520 |issue=1 |s2cid=1232881 }}</ref>

Impulsivity differentially affects disorders involving the overcontrol of food intake (such as [[anorexia nervosa]]) and disorders involving the lack of control of food intake (such as [[bulimia nervosa]]). Cognitive impulsivity, such as risk-taking, is a component of many eating disorders, including those that are restrictive.<ref name="Rosval">{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/eat.20296 |title=Impulsivity in women with eating disorders: Problem of response inhibition, planning, or attention? |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_international-journal-of-eating-disorders_2006-11_39_7/page/590 |year=2006 |last1=Rosval |first1=Lindsay |last2=Steiger |first2=Howard |last3=Bruce |first3=Kenneth |last4=Israël |first4=Mimi |last5=Richardson |first5=Jodie |last6=Aubut |first6=Melanie |journal=International Journal of Eating Disorders |volume=39 |issue=7 |pages=590–3 |pmid=16826575|doi-access=free }}</ref>  However, only people with disorders involving episodes of overeating have elevated levels of motoric impulsivity, such as reduced response inhibition capacity.<ref name="Rosval" />

One theory suggests that binging provides a short-term escape from feelings of sadness, anger, or boredom, although it may contribute to these [[negative emotion]]s in the long-term.<ref name="Vohs K.D. 2007" />  Another theory suggests that binge eating involves reward seeking, as evidenced by decreased serotonin binding receptors of binge-eating women compared to matched-weight controls<ref name="Kuikka">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s002130100716 |title=Reduced serotonin transporter binding in binge eating women |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychopharmacology_2001-05_155_3/page/310 |year=2001 |last1=Kuikka |first1=Jyrki |last2=Tammela |first2=Liisa |last3=Karhunen |first3=Leila |last4=Rissanen |first4=Aila |last5=Bergström |first5=Kim |last6=Naukkarinen |first6=Hannu |last7=Vanninen |first7=Esko |last8=Karhu |first8=Jari |last9=Lappalainen |first9=Raimo |last10=Repo-Tiihonen |first10=Eila |last11=Tiihonen |first11=Jari |last12=Uusitupa |first12=Matti |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=155 |issue=3 |pages=310–4 |pmid=11432694|s2cid=7812191 |display-authors=8 }}</ref> and predictive value of heightened reward sensitivity/drive in dysfunctional eating.<ref name="Loxton">Loxton, N.J., Dawe, S. (2004).  Alcohol abuse and dysfunctional eating in women: The contribution of reward and punishment sensitivity and family of origin. In press.{{verify source|surely not still in press nearly a decade later. was the title changed?|date=June 2013}}  As cited in: {{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.neubiorev.2004.03.007 |title=The role of impulsivity in the development of substance use and eating disorders |year=2004 |last1=Dawe |first1=Sharon |last2=Loxton |first2=Natalie J |journal=Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews |volume=28 |issue=3 |pages=343–51 |pmid=15225976 |s2cid=24435589 }}</ref>

Treatments for clinical-grade overeating include [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] to teach people how to track and change their eating habits and actions, [[interpersonal psychotherapy]] to help people analyze the contribution of their friends and family in their disorder, and pharmacological therapies including antidepressants and [[SSRIs]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Agras |first1=W. Stewart |last2=Bohon |first2=Cara |date=2021-05-07 |title=Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for the Eating Disorders |journal=Annual Review of Clinical Psychology |language=en |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=417–438 |doi=10.1146/annurev-clinpsy-081219-110907 |pmid=33962536 |s2cid=233998712 |issn=1548-5943|doi-access=free }}</ref>

=== Impulse buying ===
[[Impulse purchase|Impulse buying]] consists of purchasing a product or service without any previous intent to make that purchase.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0022-4359(99)80092-X |title=Impulse buying: Modeling its precursors |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-retailing_summer-1998_74_2/page/169 |year=1998 |last1=Beatty |first1=Sharon E. |last2=Ferrell |first2=M. Elizabeth |journal=Journal of Retailing |volume=74 |issue=2 |pages=169–91}}</ref> It has been speculated to account for as much as eighty percent of all purchases<ref>Smith, D. (1996). The joy of candy. National Petroleum News Supplement, S2.</ref> in the United States.{{Relevance inline|reason=why do we care about speculation on the percent of purchases?|date=June 2013}}

There are several theories pertaining to impulsive buying.  One theory suggests that it is exposure combining with the speed that a reward can be obtained that influences an individual to choose lesser immediate rewards over greater rewards that can be obtained later.<ref name="Mischel1972">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/h0032198 |title=Cognitive and attentional mechanisms in delay of gratification |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1972-02_21_2/page/204 |year=1972|author-link1=Walter Mischel |last1=Mischel |first1=Walter |last2=Ebbesen |first2=Ebbe B. |last3=Raskoff Zeiss |first3=Antonette |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=204–18 |pmid=5010404}}</ref> For example, a person might choose to buy a candy bar because they are in the candy aisle even though they had decided earlier that they would not buy candy while in the store.

Another theory is one of self-regulation<ref name="Vohs K.D. 2007">{{cite journal |doi=10.1086/510228 |title=Spent Resources: Self-Regulatory Resource Availability Affects Impulse Buying |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-consumer-research_2007-03_33_4/page/537 |year=2007 |last1=Vohs |first1=Kathleen D. |last2=Faber |first2=Ronald J. |journal=Journal of Consumer Research |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=537–47}}</ref> which suggests that the capacity to refrain from impulsive buying is a finite resource.  As this capacity is depleted with repeated acts of restraint susceptibility to purchasing other items on impulse increases.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

Finally, a third theory suggests an emotional and behavioral tie between the purchaser and the product which drives both the likelihood of an impulsive purchase as well as the degree that a person will retroactively be satisfied with that purchase result.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1207/s15327663jcp1603_2 |title=Reflective and Impulsive Determinants of Consumer Behavior |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-consumer-psychology_2006_16_3/page/205 |year=2006 |last1=Strack |first1=Fritz |last2=Werth |first2=Lioba |last3=Deutsch |first3=Roland |journal=Journal of Consumer Psychology |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=205–16}}</ref><ref name="Kwon, H. H. 2006">{{cite journal |first1=Harry H. |last1=Kwon |first2=Ketra L. |last2=Armstrong |year=2006 |title=Impulse Purchases of Sport Team Licensed Merchandise: What Matters? |journal=Journal of Sport Management |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=101–119 |url=http://journals.humankinetics.com/jsm-back-issues/jsmvolume20issue1january/impulsepurchasesofsportteamlicensedmerchandisewhatmatters|doi=10.1123/jsm.20.1.101 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>  Some studies have shown a large number of individuals are happy with purchases made on impulse (41% in one study<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1086/209105 |jstor=2489410 |title=The Buying Impulse |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-consumer-research_1987-09_14_2/page/189 |year=1987 |last1=Rook |first1=Dennis W. |journal=Journal of Consumer Research |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=189–99}}</ref>) which is explained as a preexisting emotional attachment which has a positive relationship both with the likelihood of initiating the purchase as well as mitigating post purchase satisfaction.<ref name="Kwon, H. H. 2006"/>  As an example, when purchasing team-related college paraphernalia a large percentage of those purchases are made on impulse and are tied to the degree with which a person has positive ties to that team.<ref name="Kwon, H. H. 2006"/>

Impulsive buying is seen both as an individual trait in which each person has a preconditioned or hereditary allotment, as well as a situational construct which is mitigated by such things as emotion in the moment of the purchase and the preconditioned ties an individual has with the product.<ref name="Vohs K.D. 2007"/><ref name="Kwon, H. H. 2006"/>

Psychotherapy and pharmacological treatments have been shown to be helpful interventions for patients with impulsive-compulsive buying disorder.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hollander |first1=Eric |last2=Stein |first2=Dan J. |title=Clinical manual of mpulse-control disorders |location=Arlington |publisher=American Psychiatric Publishing |year=2006|isbn=1585621366}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref>
Psychotherapy interventions include the use of desensitization techniques,<ref>{{cite journal |pmid=8591975 |year=1996 |last1=Bernik |first1=MA |last2=Akerman |first2=D |last3=Amaral |first3=JA |last4=Braun |first4=RC |title=Cue exposure in compulsive buying |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-clinical-psychiatry_1996-02_57_2/page/90 |volume=57 |issue=2 |pages=90 |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry}}</ref> [[self-help books]]<ref name="Catalano EM 1993">{{cite book |last1=Catalano |first1=Ellen Mohr |last2=Sonenberg |first2=Nina |title=Consuming passions: help for compulsive shoppers |location=Oakland |publisher=NewHarbinger Publications |year=1993|isbn=1879237385}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> or attending a support group.<ref name="Catalano EM 1993"/>
Pharmacological interventions include the use of [[SSRIs]], such as [[fluvoxamine]],<ref>{{cite journal |pmid=9164426 |year=1997 |last1=Black |first1=DW |last2=Monahan |first2=P |last3=Gabel |first3=J |title=Fluvoxamine in the treatment of compulsive buying |volume=58 |issue=4 |pages=159–63 |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry |doi=10.4088/JCP.v58n0404}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1023/A:1009030425631 |title=A Double-Blind Comparison of Fluvoxamine Versus Placebo in the Treatment of Compulsive Buying Disorder |year=2000 |last1=Black |first1=Donald |last2=Gabel |first2=Janelle |last3=Hansen |first3=Jeffrey |last4=Schlosser |first4=Steven |journal=Annals of Clinical Psychiatry |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=205–11 |pmid=11140921 |s2cid=189937538 }}</ref> [[citalopram]],<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.4088/JCP.v63n0808 |title=Citalopram Treatment of Compulsive Shopping |year=2002 |last1=Koran |first1=Lorrin M. |last2=Bullock |first2=Kim D. |last3=Hartston |first3=Heidi J. |last4=Elliott |first4=Michael A. |last5=d'Andrea |first5=Vincent |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry |volume=63 |issue=8 |pages=704–8 |pmid=12197451}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Guy |first1=LS |title=ECDEU assessment manual for psychopharmacology, Publication ADM 76 338 |location=Washington, DC |publisher=Department of Health, Education and Welfare |year=1976}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> [[escitalopram]],<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/01.jcp.0000264975.79367.f4 |title=Escitalopram for Compulsive Buying Disorder |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-clinical-psychopharmacology_2007-04_27_2/page/225 |year=2007 |last1=Koran |first1=Lorrin M. |last2=Aboujaoude |first2=Elias N. |last3=Solvason |first3=Brent |last4=Gamel |first4=Nona N. |last5=Smith |first5=Emily H. |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychopharmacology |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=225–7 |pmid=17414257|s2cid=41883042 }}</ref> and [[naltrexone]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.4088/JCP.v59n0403 |title=Opioid Antagonists in the Treatment of Impulse-Control Disorders |year=1998 |last1=Kim |first1=Suck Won |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry |volume=59 |issue=4 |pages=165–71 |pmid=9590666 |last2=Dwight |first2=MM |last3=McElroy |first3=SL |s2cid=7161577 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/13651500310003219 |title=Three cases of compulsive buying treated with naltrexone |year=2003 |last1=Grant |first1=Jon E. |journal=International Journal of Psychiatry in Clinical Practice |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=223–5|doi-broken-date=12 July 2025 }}</ref>

=== Impulse control disorders not elsewhere classified ===
[[Impulse control disorder]] (ICDs) are a class of [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders|DSM]] diagnoses that do not fall into the other diagnostic categories of the manual (e.g. substance use disorders), and that are characterized by extreme difficulty controlling impulses or urges despite negative consequences.<ref name="DSM IV TR" />  Individuals suffering from an impulse control disorder frequently experience five stages of symptoms: compelling urge or desire, failure to resist the urge, a heightened sense of arousal, succumbing to the urge (which usually yields relief from tension), and potential remorse or feelings of guilt after the behavior is completed.<ref name= "HollanderSteen2005">{{cite book |editor1-last=Hollander |editor1-first=E. |editor2-last=Stein |editor2-first=D.J. |year=2005 |title=Clinical manual of impulse-control disorders |pages=4–8 |location=Arlington, VA |publisher=American Psychiatric Press}}</ref> Specific disorders included within this category include [[intermittent explosive disorder]], [[kleptomania]], [[pathological gambling]], [[pyromania]], [[trichotillomania]] (hair-pulling disorder), and impulse control disorders not otherwise specified (ICD NOS). ICD NOS includes other significant difficulties that seem to be related to impulsivity but do not meet the criteria for a specific DSM diagnosis.<ref name="DSM IV TR" />

There has been much debate over whether or not the ICDs deserve a diagnostic category of their own, or whether they are in fact phenomenologically and epidemiologically related to other major psychiatric conditions like [[obsessive-compulsive disorder]] (OCD), [[Affective spectrum|affective disorders]], and [[Addiction|addictive disorders]].<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00406-006-0668-0 |title=Epidemiologic and clinical updates on impulse control disorders: A critical review |year=2006 |last1=Dell'Osso |first1=Bernardo |last2=Altamura |first2=A. Carlo |last3=Allen |first3=Andrea |last4=Marazziti |first4=Donatella |last5=Hollander |first5=Eric |journal=European Archives of Psychiatry and Clinical Neuroscience |volume=256 |issue=8 |pages=464–75 |pmid=16960655 |pmc=1705499}}</ref> In fact, the ICD classification is likely to change with the release of the [[DSM-5]] in May 2013.<ref name="DSM V Website">{{Cite web|url=http://www.dsm5.org/pages/default.aspx|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130130193514/http://www.dsm5.org/Pages/Default.aspx|url-status=dead|title=DSM-5|archive-date=January 30, 2013|website=www.dsm5.org}}</ref>  In this new revision the ICD NOS will likely be reduced or removed; proposed revisions include reclassifying trichotillomania (to be renamed hair-pulling disorder) and [[Excoriation disorder|skin-picking disorder]] as obsessive-compulsive and related disorders, moving intermittent explosive disorder under the diagnostic heading of disruptive, impulse control, and conduct disorders, and gambling disorder may be included in addiction and related disorders.<ref name="DSM V Website"/>

The role of impulsivity in the ICDs varies.  Research on kleptomania and pyromania is lacking, though there is some evidence that greater kleptomania severity is tied to poor executive functioning.<ref name= "Grant et al 2007">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.brat.2006.08.013 |title=Neuropsychological functioning in kleptomania☆ |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_behaviour-research-and-therapy_2007-07_45_7/page/1663 |year=2007 |last1=Grant |first1=J |last2=Odlaug |first2=B |last3=Wozniak |first3=J |journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy |volume=45 |issue=7 |pages=1663–70 |pmid=17007811}}</ref>

Trichotillomania and skin-picking disorder seem to be disorders that primarily involve motor impulsivity,<ref name= "Fineberg et al 2010">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/npp.2009.185 |title=Probing Compulsive and Impulsive Behaviors, from Animal Models to Endophenotypes: A Narrative Review |year=2009 |last1=Fineberg |first1=Naomi A |last2=Potenza |first2=Marc N |last3=Chamberlain |first3=Samuel R |last4=Berlin |first4=Heather A |last5=Menzies |first5=Lara |last6=Bechara |first6=Antoine |last7=Sahakian |first7=Barbara J |last8=Robbins |first8=Trevor W |last9=Bullmore |first9=Edward T |last10=Hollander |first10=Eric |journal=Neuropsychopharmacology |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=591–604 |pmid=19940844 |pmc=3055606|display-authors=8 }}</ref><ref name= "Odlaug 2010">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.pnpbp.2009.11.008 |title=Motor inhibition and cognitive flexibility in pathologic skin picking |year=2010 |last1=Odlaug |first1=Brian L. |last2=Chamberlain |first2=Samuel R. |last3=Grant |first3=Jon E. |journal=Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology and Biological Psychiatry |volume=34 |pages=208–11 |pmid=19913592 |issue=1 |s2cid=207409063 }}</ref> and will likely be classified in the DSM-5 within the obsessive-compulsive and related disorders category.<ref name="DSM V Website"/>

Pathological gambling, in contrast, seems to involve many diverse aspects of impulsivity and abnormal [[Reward system|reward circuitry]]  (similar to substance use disorders) that has led to it being increasingly conceptualized as a non-substance or [[behavioral addiction]].<ref name="Leeman Pot 2012">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00213-011-2550-7 |title=Similarities and differences between pathological gambling and substance use disorders: A focus on impulsivity and compulsivity |year=2011 |last1=Leeman |first1=Robert F. |last2=Potenza |first2=Marc N. |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=219 |issue=2 |pages=469–90 |pmid=22057662 |pmc=3249521}}</ref> Evidence elucidating the role of impulsivity in pathological gambling is accumulating, with pathological gambling samples demonstrating greater [[#Go/no-go and stop-signal reaction time tasks|response impulsivity]], [[#5-Choice Serial Reaction Time Task (5-CSRTT) and Differential Reinforcement of Low rates (DRL)|choice impulsivity]], and reflection impulsivity than comparison control samples.<ref name="Leeman Pot 2012"/> Additionally, pathological gamblers tend to demonstrate greater response perseveration (compulsivity) and risky decisionmaking in laboratory gambling tasks compared to controls, though there is no strong evidence suggesting that attention and [[working memory]] are impaired in pathological gamblers.<ref name="Leeman Pot 2012"/> These relations between impulsivity and pathological gambling are confirmed by brain function research: pathological gamblers demonstrate less activation in the frontal cortical regions (implicated in impulsivity) compared to controls during behavioral tasks tapping response impulsivity, compulsivity, and risk/reward.<ref name="Leeman Pot 2012"/>  Preliminary, though variable, findings also suggest that striatal activation is different between gamblers and controls, and that [[neurotransmitter]] differences (e.g. [[dopamine]], [[serotonin]], [[opioids]], [[glutamate]], [[norepinephrine]]) may exist as well.<ref name="Leeman Pot 2012"/>

Individuals with intermittent explosive disorder, also known as impulsive aggression, have exhibited serotonergic abnormalities and show differential activation in response to emotional stimuli and situations.<ref name="Coccaro 2012">{{cite journal |doi=10.1176/appi.ajp.2012.11081259 |title=Intermittent Explosive Disorder as a Disorder of Impulsive Aggression for DSM-5 |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_american-journal-of-psychiatry_2012-06_169_6/page/577 |year=2012 |last1=Coccaro |first1=Emil F. |journal=American Journal of Psychiatry |pmid=22535310 |volume=169 |issue=6 |pages=577–88}}</ref>  Notably, intermittent explosive disorder is not associated with a higher likelihood of diagnosis with any of the other ICDs but is highly comorbid with disruptive behavior disorders in childhood.<ref name="Coccaro 2012"/>  Intermittent explosive disorder is likely to be re-classified in the DSM-5 under the heading of disruptive, impulse control, and conduct disorders.<ref name="DSM V Website"/>

These sorts of impulse control disorders are most often [[#Treatment of specific disorders of impulsivity|treated]] using certain types of psychopharamcological interventions (e.g. antidepressants) and behavioral treatments like [[cognitive behavioral therapy]].{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

== Theories of impulsivity ==

=== Ego (cognitive) depletion ===
According to the ego (or cognitive) depletion theory of impulsivity, [[self-control]] refers to the capacity for altering one's own responses, especially to bring them into line with standards such as ideals, values, morals, and social expectations, and to support the pursuit of long-term goals.<ref name="Baumeister 2007">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8721.2007.00534.x |title=The Strength Model of Self-Control |year=2007 |last1=Baumeister |first1=Roy F. |last2=Vohs |first2=Kathleen D. |last3=Tice |first3=Dianne M. |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |volume=16 |issue=6 |pages=351–5|s2cid=7414142 }}</ref> Self-control enables a person to restrain or override one response, thereby making a different response possible.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" />
A major tenet of the theory is that engaging in acts of self-control draws from a limited "reservoir" of self-control that, when depleted, results in reduced capacity for further self-regulation.<ref name="Baumeister 1998">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.74.5.1252 |title=Ego depletion: Is the active self a limited resource? |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1998-05_74_5/page/1252 |year=1998 |last1=Baumeister |first1=Roy F. |last2=Bratslavsky |first2=Ellen |last3=Muraven |first3=Mark |last4=Tice |first4=Dianne M. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=74 |issue=5 |pages=1252–65 |pmid=9599441|bibcode=1998JPSP...74.1252B |s2cid=14627317 }}</ref><ref name="Muraven 2000">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.126.2.247 |title=Self-regulation and depletion of limited resources: Does self-control resemble a muscle? |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-bulletin_2000-03_126_2/page/247 |year=2000 |last1=Muraven |first1=Mark |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=126 |issue=2 |pages=247–59 |pmid=10748642 |bibcode=2000PsycB.126..247M |s2cid=10962693 }}</ref> Self-control is viewed as analogous to a muscle: Just as a muscle requires strength and energy to exert force over a period of time, acts that have high self-control demands also require strength and energy to perform.<ref name="Hagger 2010">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/a0019486 |title=Ego depletion and the strength model of self-control: A meta-analysis |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-bulletin_2010-07_136_4/page/495 |year=2010 |last1=Hagger |first1=Martin S. |last2=Wood |first2=Chantelle |last3=Stiff |first3=Chris |last4=Chatzisarantis |first4=Nikos L. D. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=136 |issue=4 |pages=495–525 |pmid=20565167|s2cid=22705578 }}</ref> Similarly, as muscles become fatigued after a period of sustained exertion and have reduced capacity to exert further force, self-control can also become depleted when demands are made of self-control resources over a period of time. Baumeister and colleagues termed the state of diminished self-control strength [[ego depletion]] (or cognitive depletion).<ref name="Muraven 2000" />

The strength model of self-control asserts that:
*Just as exercise can make muscles stronger, there are signs that regular exertions of self-control can improve willpower strength.<ref name="Baumeister 2006">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1467-6494.2006.00428.x |title=Self-Regulation and Personality: How Interventions Increase Regulatory Success, and How Depletion Moderates the Effects of Traits on Behavior |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality_2006-12_74_6/page/1773 |year=2006 |last1=Baumeister |first1=Roy F. |last2=Gailliot |first2=Matthew |last3=Dewall |first3=C. Nathan |last4=Oaten |first4=Megan |journal=Journal of Personality |volume=74 |issue=6 |pages=1773–801 |pmid=17083666}}</ref> These improvements typically take the form of resistance to depletion, in the sense that performance at self-control tasks deteriorates at a slower rate.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" /> Targeted efforts to control behavior in one area, such as spending money or exercise, lead to improvements in unrelated areas, such as studying or household chores. And daily exercises in self-control, such as improving posture, altering verbal behavior, and using one's nondominant hand for simple tasks, gradually produce improvements in self-control as measured by laboratory tasks.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" /> The finding that these improvements carry over into tasks vastly different from the daily exercises shows that the improvements are not due to simply increasing skill or acquiring [[self-efficacy]] from practice.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" />
*Just as athletes begin to conserve their remaining strength when their muscles begin to tire, so do self-controllers when some of their self-regulatory resources have been expended. The severity of behavioral impairment during depletion depends in part on whether the person expects further challenges and demands.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" /> When people expect to have to exert self-control later, they will curtail current performance more severely than if no such demands are anticipated.<ref name="Muraven 2006">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.91.3.524 |title=Conserving self-control strength |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_2006-09_91_3/page/524 |year=2006 |last1=Muraven |first1=Mark |last2=Shmueli |first2=Dikla |last3=Burkley |first3=Edward |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=524–37 |pmid=16938035}}</ref>
*Consistent with the conservation hypothesis, people can exert self-control despite ego depletion if the stakes are high enough. Offering cash incentives or other motives for good performance counteracts the effects of ego depletion.<ref name="Muraven 2003">{{cite journal |doi=10.1177/0146167203029007008 |title=Mechanisms of Self-Control Failure: Motivation and Limited Resources |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_personality-and-social-psychology-bulletin_2003-07_29_7/page/894 |year=2003 |last1=Muraven |first1=Mark |last2=Slessareva |first2=Elisaveta |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |volume=29 |issue=7 |pages=894–906 |pmid=15018677 |s2cid=16961238 }}</ref> This may seem surprising but in fact it may be highly adaptive. Given the value and importance of the capacity for self-control, it would be dangerous for a person to lose that capacity completely, and so ego depletion effects may occur because people start conserving their remaining strength.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" /> When people do exert themselves on the second task, they deplete the resource even more, as reflected in severe impairments on a third task that they have not anticipated.<ref name="Muraven 2006" />

Empirical tests of the ego-depletion effect typically adopt [[dual-task paradigm]].<ref name="Baumeister 1998" /><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.74.3.774 |title=Self-control as a limited resource: Regulatory depletion patterns |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1998-03_74_3/page/774 |year=1998 |last1=Muraven |first1=Mark |last2=Tice |first2=Dianne M. |last3=Baumeister |first3=Roy F. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=774–89 |pmid=9523419}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.91.3.456 |title=High-maintenance interaction: Inefficient social coordination impairs self-regulation |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_2006-09_91_3/page/456 |year=2006 |last1=Finkel |first1=Eli J. |last2=Campbell |first2=W. Keith |last3=Brunell |first3=Amy B. |last4=Dalton |first4=Amy N. |last5=Scarbeck |first5=Sarah J. |last6=Chartrand |first6=Tanya L. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=456–75 |pmid=16938030}}</ref> Participants assigned to an experimental ego-depletion group are required to engage in two consecutive tasks requiring self-control.<ref name="Hagger 2010" /> Control participants are also required to engage in two consecutive tasks, but only the second task requires self-control. The strength model predicts that the performance of the experimental-group on the second self-control task will be impaired relative to that of the control group. This is because the finite self-control resources of the experimental participants will be diminished after the initial self-control task, leaving little to draw on for the second task.<ref name="Baumeister 2007" />

The effects of ego depletion do not appear to be a product of mood or arousal. In most studies, mood and arousal has not been found to differ between participants who exerted self-control and those who did not.<ref name="Baumeister 1998" /><ref name="Mark 2012">{{cite book |last=Muraven |first=Mark |chapter=Ego Depletion: Theory and Evidence |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oqsezTrzKZEC&pg=PA111 |pages=111–26 |editor1-first=Richard M. |editor1-last=Ryan |title=The Oxford Handbook of Human Motivation |year=2012 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn=978-0-19-539982-0}}</ref> Likewise, mood and arousal was not related to final self-control performance.<ref name="Mark 2012" /> The same is true for more specific mood items, such as frustration, irritation, annoyance, boredom, or interest as well. Feedback about success and failure of the self-control efforts does not appear to affect performance.<ref name="Wallace 2002">{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/152988602317232786 |title=The Effects of Success versus Failure Feedback on Further Self-Control |year=2002 |last1=Wallace |first1=Harry M. |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |journal=Self and Identity |volume=1 |pages=35–41|s2cid=144797743 }}</ref> In short, the decline in self-control performance after exerting self-control appears to be directly related to the amount of self-control exerted and cannot be easily explained by other, well-established psychological processes.<ref name="Mark 2012" />

=== Automatic vs. controlled processes/cognitive control ===

[[Dual process theory]] states that mental processes operate in two separate classes: automatic and controlled.  In general, automatic processes are those that are experiential in nature, occur without involving higher levels of cognition,<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.3200/JRLP.143.3.293-317 |title=College Students' Awareness of Irrational Judgments on Gambling Tasks: A Dual-Process Account |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-psychology_2009-05_143_3/page/293 |year=2009 |last1=Amsel |first1=Eric |last2=Close |first2=Jason |last3=Sadler |first3=Eric |last4=Klaczynski |first4=Paul A. |journal=The Journal of Psychology |volume=143 |issue=3 |pages=293–317 |pmid=19455857|s2cid=1997168 }}</ref> and are based on prior experiences or informal heuristics. Controlled decisions are effortful and largely conscious processes in which an individual weighs alternatives and makes a more deliberate decision.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}
*''Automatic Process'':  Automatic processes have four main features.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite book |first1=Bertram |last1=Gawronski |first2=Laura A. |last2=Creighton |year=2013 |chapter=Dual-Process Theories |pages=218–312 |editor1-first=Donal E. |editor1-last=Carlston |title=The Oxford Handbook of Social Cognition |publisher=Oup USA |isbn=978-0-19-973001-8}}</ref> They occur unintentionally or without a conscious decision, the cost of the decision is very low in mental resources, they cannot be easily stopped, and they occur without conscious thought on the part of the individual making them.
*''Controlled Process'':  Controlled processes also have four main features<ref name="ReferenceA"/> that are very close to the opposite in spectrum from their automatic counterparts.  Controlled processes occur intentionally, they require the expenditure of cognitive resources, the individual making the decision can stop the process voluntarily, and the mental process is a conscious one.

Dual process theories at one time considered any single action/thought as either being automatic or controlled.<ref name="ReferenceA"/>  However, currently they are seen as operating more along a continuum as most impulsive actions will have both controlled and automatic attributes.<ref name="ReferenceA"/>  Automatic processes are classified according to whether they are meant to inhibit or to facilitate a thought process.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-295X.115.2.314 |title=The self-regulation of automatic associations and behavioral impulses |year=2008 |last1=Sherman |first1=Jeffrey W. |last2=Gawronski |first2=Bertram |last3=Gonsalkorale |first3=Karen |last4=Hugenberg |first4=Kurt |last5=Allen |first5=Thomas J. |last6=Groom |first6=Carla J. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=115 |issue=2 |pages=314–35 |pmid=18426292|s2cid=20186915 |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/4x59z6nr }}</ref> For example, in one study<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.63.4.534 |title=Cognitive-experiential self-theory and subjective probability: Further evidence for two conceptual systems |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1992-10_63_4/page/534 |year=1992 |last1=Kirkpatrick |first1=Lee A. |last2=Epstein |first2=Seymour |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=63 |issue=4 |pages=534–44 |pmid=1447684}}</ref> researchers offered individuals a choice between a 1 in 10 chance of winning a prize and a 10 in 100 chance. Many participants chose one of the choices over the other without identifying that the chances inherent in each were the same as they saw either only 10 chances total as more beneficial, or of having 10 chances to win as more beneficial. In effect impulsive decisions can be made as prior information and experiences dictate one of the courses of action is more beneficial when in actuality careful consideration would better enable the individual to make a more informed and improved decision.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

=== Intertemporal choice ===

[[Intertemporal choice]] is defined as "decisions with consequences that play out over time".<ref name="Berns">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.tics.2007.08.011 |url=http://nrs.harvard.edu/urn-3:HUL.InstRepos:4554332 |title=Intertemporal choice – toward an integrative framework |year=2007 |last1=Berns |first1=Gregory S. |last2=Laibson |first2=David |last3=Loewenstein |first3=George |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |volume=11 |issue=11 |pages=482–8 |pmid=17980645|s2cid=22282339 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>  This is often assessed using the relative value people assign to rewards at different points in time, either by asking experimental subjects to choose between alternatives or examining behavioral choices in a naturalistic setting.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

Intertemporal choice is commonly measured in the laboratory using a "delayed discounting" paradigm, which measures the process of devaluing rewards and punishments that happen in the future.<ref name="Berns" /> In this paradigm, subjects must choose between a smaller reward delivered soon and a larger reward delivered at a delay in the future.  Choosing the smaller-sooner reward is considered impulsive. By repeatedly making these choices, indifference points can be estimated. For example, if someone chose $70 now over $100 in a week, but chose the $100 in a week over $60 now, it can be inferred that they are indifferent between $100 in a week and an intermediate value between $60 and $70.  A delay discounting curve can be obtained for each participant by plotting their indifference points with different reward amounts and time delays.  Individual differences in discounting curves are affected by personality characteristics such as self-reports of impulsivity and [[locus of control]]; personal characteristics such as age, gender, IQ, race, and culture; socioeconomic characteristics such as income and education; and many other variables.<ref name=Hampton2018>{{cite journal | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2018.01545 | volume=9 | issue= 1545 | title=Things for Those Who Wait: Predictive Modeling Highlights Importance of Delay Discounting for Income Attainment | year=2018 | journal=Frontiers in Psychology | last1 = Hampton | first1 = W.| page=1545 | pmid=30233449 | pmc=6129952 | doi-access=free }}</ref> to drug addiction.<ref name=MacKillop2011>{{cite journal | doi = 10.1007/s00213-011-2229-0| volume=216 | issue= 3 | title=Delayed reward discounting and addictive behavior | year=2011 | journal=Psychopharmacology | last1 = MacKillop | first1 = J.| pages=305–321 | pmid=21373791 | pmc=3201846 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1037/12069-002  |title= Impulsivity: The behavioral and neurological science of discounting|year=2010 |last1=Odum |first1=Amy L. |last2=Baumann |first2=Ana A. L. |isbn=978-1-4338-0477-9 |pages=39–65 |editor1-last=Madden |editor1-first=Gregory J. |editor2-last=Bickel |editor2-first=Warren K. |chapter= Delay discounting: State and trait variable}}</ref> Lesions of the [[nucleus accumbens core]] subregion<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1126/science.1060818 |title=Impulsive Choice Induced in Rats by Lesions of the Nucleus Accumbens Core |year=2001 |last1=Cardinal |first1=R. N. |journal=Science |volume=292 |issue=5526 |pages=2499–501 |pmid=11375482 |last2=Pennicott |first2=DR |last3=Sugathapala |first3=CL |last4=Robbins |first4=TW |last5=Everitt |first5=BJ |s2cid=18020488 |doi-access=free }}</ref> or [[basolateral amygdala]]<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.5606-03.2004 |title=Contrasting Roles of Basolateral Amygdala and Orbitofrontal Cortex in Impulsive Choice |year=2004 |last1=Winstanley|first1=C. A. |journal=Journal of Neuroscience |volume=24 |issue=20 |pages=4718–22 |pmid=15152031 |pmc=6729470 |last2=Theobald |first2=DE |last3=Cardinal |first3=RN |last4=Robbins |first4=TW|author1-link=Catharine Winstanley }}</ref> produce shifts towards choosing the smaller-sooner reward, suggesting the involvement of these brain regions in the preference for delayed reinforcers.  There is also evidence that the orbitofrontal cortex is involved in delay discounting, although there is currently debate on whether lesions in this region result in more or less impulsivity.<ref name="dalley" />

Economic theory suggests that optimal discounting involves the [[exponential discounting]] of value over time.  This model assumes that people and institutions should discount the value of rewards and punishments at a constant rate according to how delayed they are in time.<ref name="Berns" />  While economically rational, recent evidence suggests that people and animals do not discount exponentially.  Many studies suggest that humans and animals discount future values according to a [[hyperbolic discounting]] curve where the discount factor decreases with the length of the delay (for example, waiting from today to tomorrow involves more loss of value than waiting from twenty days to twenty-one days).  Further evidence for non-constant delay discounting is suggested by the differential involvement of various brain regions in evaluating immediate versus delayed consequences.  Specifically, the [[prefrontal cortex]] is activated when choosing between rewards at a short delay or a long delay, but regions associated with the dopamine system are additionally activated when the option of an immediate reinforcer is added.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1126/science.1100907 |title=Separate Neural Systems Value Immediate and Delayed Monetary Rewards |year=2004 |last1=McClure |first1=S. M. |journal=Science |volume=306 |issue=5695 |pages=503–7 |pmid=15486304 |last2=Laibson |first2=DI |last3=Loewenstein |first3=G |last4=Cohen |first4=JD |bibcode=2004Sci...306..503M |s2cid=14663380 }}</ref> Additionally, intertemporal choices differ from economic models because they involve anticipation (which may involve a neurological "reward" even if the reinforcer is delayed), self-control (and the breakdown of it when faced with temptations), and representation (how the choice is framed may influence desirability of the reinforcer),<ref name="Berns" /> none of which are accounted for by a model that assumes economic rationality.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

One facet of intertemporal choice is the possibility for preference reversal, when a tempting reward becomes more highly valued than abstaining only when immediately available.<ref name = "M&G10" />  For example, when sitting home alone, a person may report that they value the health benefit of not smoking a cigarette over the effect of smoking one.  However, later at night when the cigarette is immediately available, their subjective value of the cigarette may rise and they may choose to smoke it.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

A theory called the "primrose path" is intended to explain how preference reversal can lead to addiction in the long run.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Herrnstein |first1=Richard J. |last2=Prelec |first2=Drazen |year=1992 |chapter=A Theory of Addiction |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3CarEZZfdkUCpg |editor1-first=George |editor1-last=Loewenstein |editor2-first=Jon |editor2-last=Elster |title=Choice Over Time |pages=331–60 |location=New York |publisher=Russell Sage Foundation |isbn=978-0-87154-558-9 }}{{Dead link|date=September 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> As an example, a lifetime of sobriety may be more highly valued than a lifetime of alcoholism, but, at the same time, one drink now may be more highly valued than not drinking now.  Because it is always "now," the drink is always chosen, and a paradoxical effect occurs whereby the more-valued long-term alternative is not achieved because the more-valued short-term alternative is always chosen. This is an example of complex ambivalence,<ref>{{cite book |last=Rachlin |first=Howard |chapter=Complex Ambivalence |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KWp-EJaEAUIC&pg=PA57 |pages=57–81 |title=The Science of Self-Control |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts |year=2000 |isbn=978-0-674-00093-3}}</ref> when a choice is made not between two concrete alternatives but between one immediate and tangible alternative (i.e. having a drink) and one delayed and abstract alternative (i.e. sobriety).

Similarities between humans and non-human animals in intertemporal choice have been studied.  Pigeons<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.3758/BF03209777 |title=Preference reversal and delayed reinforcement |year=1981 |last1=Ainslie |first1=George |last2=Herrnstein |first2=R.
