J. |journal=Animal Learning & Behavior |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=476–82|s2cid=145693802 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and rats<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1901/jeab.2003.79-233 |title=Preference reversals with food and water reinforcers in rats |year=2003 |last1=Green |first1=Leonard |last2=Estle |first2=Sara J |journal=Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior |volume=79 |issue=2 |pages=233–42 |pmid=12822689 |pmc=1284932}}</ref> also discount hyperbolically; tamarin monkeys do not wait more than eight seconds to triple the amount of a food reward.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2004.0285 |title=The ecology and evolution of patience in two New World monkeys |year=2005 |last1=Hauser |first1=Marc D. |last2=Hallinan |first2=Elizabeth V. |last3=Stevens |first3=Jeffrey R. |journal=Biology Letters |volume=1 |issue=2 |pages=223–6 |pmid=17148172 |pmc=1626214}}</ref> The question arises as to whether this is a difference of homology or analogy—that is, whether the same underlying process underlies human-animal similarities or whether different processes are manifesting in similar patterns of results.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

=== Inhibitory control ===
{{main|inhibitory control}}

Inhibitory control, often conceptualized as an [[executive functions|executive function]], is the ability to [[cognitive inhibition|inhibit or hold back a prepotent response]].<ref name="Logan et al 1997" >{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1997.tb00545.x |title=Impulsivity and Inhibitory Control |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-science_1997-01_8_1/page/60 |year=1997 |last1=Logan |first1=Gordon D. |last2=Schachar |first2=Russell J. |last3=Tannock |first3=Rosemary |journal=Psychological Science |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=60–4 |jstor=40062847|s2cid=145540424 }}</ref> It is theorized that impulsive behavior reflects a deficit in this ability to inhibit a response; impulsive people may find it more difficult to inhibit action whereas non-impulsive people may find it easier to do so.<ref name="Logan et al 1997" /> There is evidence that, in normal adults, commonly used behavioral measures of inhibitory control correlate with standard self-report measures of impulsivity.<ref name="Enticott et al 2006" >{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2006.01.011 |title=Associations between laboratory measures of executive inhibitory control and self-reported impulsivity |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_personality-and-individual-differences_2006-07_41_2/page/n92 |year=2006 |last1=Enticott |first1=Peter G. |last2=Ogloff |first2=James R.P. |last3=Bradshaw |first3=John L. |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=41 |issue=2 |pages=285–94}}</ref>

Inhibitory control may itself be multifaceted, evidenced by numerous distinct inhibition constructs that can be measured in different ways, and relate to specific types of psychopathology.<ref name="Nigg 2000" >{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.126.2.220 |title=On inhibition/disinhibition in developmental psychopathology: Views from cognitive and personality psychology and a working inhibition taxonomy |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-bulletin_2000-03_126_2/page/220 |year=2000 |last1=Nigg |first1=Joel T. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=126 |issue=2 |pages=220–46 |pmid=10748641}}</ref> [[Joel Nigg]] developed a useful working taxonomy of these different types of inhibition, drawing heavily from the fields of cognitive and personality psychology<ref name="Nigg 2000" /> Nigg's eight proposed types of  inhibition include the following:

==== Executive inhibition ====

===== Interference control =====
Suppression of a stimulus that elicits an interfering response, enabling a person to complete the primary response.  Interference control can also refer to suppressing distractors.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

Interference control has been measured using cognitive tasks like the [[Stroop effect|stroop test]], [[Eriksen flanker task|flanker tasks]], [[Dual-task paradigm|dual task interference]], and [[priming (psychology)|priming]] tasks.<ref name="GrattonColesDonchin-1992" >{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0096-3445.121.4.480 |title=Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses |year=1992 |last1=Gratton |first1=Gabriele |last2=Coles |first2=Michael G. H. |last3=Donchin |first3=Emanuel |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |volume=121 |issue=4 |pages=480–506|pmid=1431740 }}</ref>  Personality researchers have used the Rothbart effortful control measures and the [[conscientiousness]] scale of the [[Big Five personality traits|Big Five]] as inventory measures of interference control.  Based on imaging and neural research it is theorized that the [[Anterior cingulate cortex|anterior cingulate]], the [[Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex|dorsolateral prefrontal/premotor cortex]], and the [[basal ganglia]] are related to interference control.<ref name="Diamond1997" >{{cite conference |last=Diamond |first=A. |date=May 1997 |title=Executive functions: Developmental perspective |publisher=University of Toronto Division of Child Psychiatry |conference=Executive Function and Developmental Psychopathology |location=Toronto, Canada}}</ref><ref name="CabezaNyberg1997" >{{cite journal |doi=10.1162/jocn.1997.9.1.1 |pmid=23968177 |title=Imaging Cognition: An Empirical Review of PET Studies with Normal Subjects |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-cognitive-neuroscience_1997-01_9_1/page/n4 |year=1997 |last1=Cabeza |first1=Roberto |last2=Nyberg |first2=Lars |journal=Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=1–26 |s2cid=33664797 }}</ref>

===== Cognitive inhibition =====
[[Cognitive inhibition]] is the suppression of unwanted or irrelevant thoughts to protect working memory and attention resources.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

Cognitive inhibition is most often measured through tests of directed ignoring, self-report on one's intrusive thoughts, and negative priming tasks.  As with interference control, personality psychologists have measured cognitive inhibition using the Rothbart Effortful Control scale and the Big Five Conscientiousness scale. The [[anterior cingulate]], the prefrontal regions, and the association cortex seem to be involved in cognitive inhibition.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

===== Behavioral inhibition =====
Behavioral Inhibition is the suppression of prepotent response.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

Behavioral inhibition is usually measured using the Go/No Go task, Stop signal task, and reports of suppression of attentional orienting.  Surveys that are theoretically relevant to behavioral inhibition include the Rothbart effortful control scale, and the [[Big Five personality traits|Big Five]] Conscientiousness dimension.<ref name="Nigg 2000" /> The rationale behind the use of behavioral measures like the Stop signal task is that "go" processes and "stop processes" are independent, and that, upon "go" and "stop" cues, they "race" against each other; if the go process wins the race, the prepotent response is executed, whereas if the stop processes wins the race, the response is withheld. In this context, impulsivity is conceptualized as a relatively slow stop process.<ref name="Logan et al 1984">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.10.2.276 |title=On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method |year=1984 |last1=Logan |first1=Gordon D. |last2=Cowan |first2=William B. |last3=Davis |first3=Kenneth A. |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=276–91|pmid=6232345 |s2cid=2595498 }}</ref> The brain regions involved in behavioral inhibition appear to be the lateral and orbital prefrontal regions along with premotor processes.

===== Oculomotor inhibition =====
Oculomotor inhibition is the effortful suppression of [[Saccade|reflexive saccade]].<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

Oculomotor inhibition is tested using antisaccade and oculomotor tasks. Also, the Rothbart effortful control measure and the Big Five Conscientiousness dimension are thought to tap some of the effortful processes underlying the ability to suppress saccade.  The frontal eye fields and the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex are involved in oculomotor inhibition.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

==== Motivational inhibition ====

===== In response to punishment =====
Motivational inhibition and response in the face of punishment can be measured using tasks tapping inhibition of primary response, modified go/no go tasks, inhibition of competing response, and [[Emotional Stroop test|emotional Stroop]] tasks.<ref name="Nigg 2000" /> Personality psychologists also use the [[Gray's biopsychological theory of personality|Gray]] behavioral inhibition system measure, the [[Eysenck Personality Questionnaire|Eysenck]] scale for neurotic introversion, and the [[Alternative five model of personality|Zuckerman]] Neuroticism-Anxiety scale.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />  The  Septal-hippocampal formation, cingulate, and motor systems seem to be the brain areas most involved in response to punishment.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

===== In response to novelty =====
Response to novelty has been measured using the Kagan behavioral inhibition system measure and scales of neurotic introversion.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />  The amygdaloid system is implicated in novelty response.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

==== Automatic inhibition of attention ====

===== Recently inspected stimuli =====
Suppression of recently inspected stimuli for both attention and oculomotor saccade is usually measured using attentional and oculomotor inhibition of return tests. The superior colliculus and the midbrain, oculomotor pathway are involved in suppression of stimuli.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

===== Neglected stimuli =====
Information at locations that are not presently being attended to is suppressed, while attending elsewhere.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

This involves measures of covert attentional orienting and neglect, along with personality scales on neuroticism.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />  The posterior association cortex and subcortical pathways are implicated in this sort of inhibition.<ref name="Nigg 2000" />

=== Action/Inaction goals ===
Recent psychology research also yields out the condition of impulsivity in relation to peoples' general goal setting. It is possible these action and inaction goals are underlying people's behavioral differences in their daily lives since they can demonstrate "patterns comparable to natural variation in overall activity levels".<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Albarracin | first1 = D. | last2 = Handley | first2 = I. M. | last3 = Noguchi | first3 = K. | last4 = McCulloch | first4 = K. C. | last5 = Li | first5 = H. | last6 = Leeper | first6 = J. | last7 = Brown | first7 = R. D. | last8 = Earl | first8 = A. | last9 = Hart | first9 = W. | year = 2008 | title = Increasing and decreasing motor and cognitive output: A model of general action and inaction goals | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 95 | issue = 3| pages = 510–523 | doi=10.1037/a0012833 | pmid=18729691 | pmc=3626413}}</ref> More specifically, the level of impulsivity and mania people have might positive correlated with favorable attitudes about and goals of general action while negatively respond to favorable attitudes about and goals of general inaction.

== Assessment of impulsivity ==

=== Personality tests and reports ===

==== Barratt Impulsiveness Scale ====
The [[Barratt Impulsiveness Scale]] (BIS) is one of the oldest and most widely used measures of impulsive personality traits. The first BIS was developed in 1959 by Dr. Ernest Barratt.<ref name="Barratt 1959">{{cite journal |doi=10.2466/pms.1959.9.3.191 |title=Anxiety and Impulsiveness Related to Psychomotor Efficiency |year=1959 |last1=Barratt |first1=Ernest S. |journal=Perceptual and Motor Skills |volume=9 |issue=3 |pages=191–8|s2cid=143118554 }}</ref> It has been revised extensively to achieve two major goals: (1) to identify a set of "impulsiveness" items that was orthogonal to a set of "anxiety" items as measured by the Taylor Manifest Anxiety Scale (MAS) or the Cattell Anxiety Scale, and (2) to define impulsiveness within the structure of related personality traits like Eysenck's Extraversion dimension or Zuckerman's Sensation-Seeking dimension, especially the disinhibition subfactor.<ref name="Barratt 1959" /> The BIS-11 with 30 items was developed in 1995.<ref name="Patton 1995">{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/1097-4679(199511)51:6<768::AID-JCLP2270510607>3.0.CO;2-1 |title=Factor structure of the barratt impulsiveness scale |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-clinical-psychology_1995-11_51_6/page/768 |year=1995 |last1=Patton |first1=Jim H. |last2=Stanford |first2=Matthew S. |last3=Barratt |first3=Ernest S. |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=51 |issue=6 |pages=768–74 |pmid=8778124}}</ref> According to Patton and colleagues, there are 3 subscales (Attentional Impulsiveness, Motor Impulsiveness, and Non-Planning Impulsiveness) with six factors:<ref name="Patton 1995" />
#[[Attention]]: "focusing on a task at hand".
#Motor impulsiveness: "acting on the spur of the moment".
#[[Self-control]]: "planning and thinking carefully".
#Cognitive complexity: "enjoying challenging mental tasks".
#[[Perseveration|Perseverance]]: "a consistent life style".
#Cognitive instability: "thought insertion and racing thoughts".

==== Eysenck Impulsiveness Scale ====
The Eysenck Impulsiveness Scale (EIS)<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/0191-8869(85)90011-X |title=Age norms for impulsiveness, venturesomeness and empathy in adults |year=1985 |last1=Eysenck |first1=Sybil B.G. |last2=Pearson |first2=P.R. |last3=Easting |first3=G. |last4=Allsopp |first4=J.F. |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=6 |issue=5 |pages=613–9}}</ref> is a 54-item yes/no questionnaire designed to measure impulsiveness. Three subscales are computed from this measure: Impulsiveness, Venturesomeness, and Empathy. Impulsiveness is defined as "behaving without thinking and without realizing the risk involved in the behavior".<ref name="Chelsea 2006">{{cite journal |last=Dean |first=Chelsea R. |title=Assessing self-injurious behaviors on a College Campus |journal=Senior Honors Theses & Projects |year=2006 |series=Senior Honor Theses: Paper 59 |url=http://commons.emich.edu/honors/59/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203021934/http://commons.emich.edu/honors/59/ |archive-date=2013-12-03 }}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> Venturesomeness is conceptualized as "being conscious of the risk of the behavior but acting anyway".<ref name="Chelsea 2006" /> The questionnaire was constructed through factor analysis to contain items that most highly loaded on impulsiveness and venturesomeness.<ref name="Chelsea 2006" /> The EIS is a widely used and well-validated measure.<ref name="Chelsea 2006" />

==== Dickman Impulsivity Inventory ====
The Dickman Impulsivity Inventory was first developed in 1990 by Scott J. Dickman. This scale is based on Dickman's proposal that there are two types of impulsivity that are significantly different from one another.<ref name = Burnett>{{cite journal | title=Impulsivity and Rapid Decision-Making for Reward | author=Burnett Heyes S., Adam RJ., Urner M., van der Leer L., Bahrami B., Bays PM., Husain M. | journal=Front. Psychol.|date= 22 May 2012 | pmc=3357492 | pmid=22661960 | doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2012.00153 | volume=3 | pages=153| last2=Adam | last3=Urner | last4=Van Der Leer | last5=Bahrami | last6=Bays | last7=Husain | doi-access=free }}</ref> This includes [[functional impulsivity]] which is characterized by quick decision making when it is optimal, a trait that is often considered to be a source of pride. The scale also includes [[dysfunctional impulsivity]] which is characterized by making quick decisions when it is not optimal. This type of impulsivity is most often associated with life difficulties including substance abuse problems and other negative outcomes.<ref name="Dickman">Dickman, S. J. (1990). Functional and dysfunctional impulsivity: Personality and cognitive correlates. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 58(1), 95–102.</ref>

This scale includes 63 items of which 23 are related to dysfunctional impulsivity, 17 are related to functional impulsivity, and 23 are filler questions that relate to neither construct.<ref name="Dickman" /> This scale has been developed into a version for use with children<ref>Brunas-Wagstaff, J., Tilley, A., Verity, M., Ford, S., & Thompson, D. (1997). Functional and dysfunctional impulsivity in children and their relationship to Eysenck's impulsiveness and venturesomeness dimensions. ''Personality and Individual Differences'', 22(1), 19–35.</ref> as well as into several languages. Dickman showed there is no correlation between these two tendencies across individuals, and they also have different cognitive correlates.<ref name = Burnett/>

==== UPPS Impulsive Behavior Scale ====
The UPPS Impulsive Behavior Scale<ref name="Whiteside 2001">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(00)00064-7 |title=The Five Factor Model and impulsivity: Using a structural model of personality to understand impulsivity |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_personality-and-individual-differences_2001-03_30_4/page/669 |year=2001 |last1=Whiteside |first1=Stephen P. |last2=Lynam |first2=Donald R. |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=669–89|s2cid=43839830 }}</ref> is a 45-item self-report questionnaire that was designed to measure impulsivity across dimensions of the Five Factor Model of personality. The UPPS includes 4 sub-scales: lack of premeditation, urgency, lack of perseverance, and sensation-seeking.

UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale (UPPS-P)<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/1040-3590.19.1.107 |title=Integration of impulsivity and positive mood to predict risky behavior: Development and validation of a measure of positive urgency |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-assessment_2007-03_19_1/page/107 |year=2007 |last1=Cyders |first1=Melissa A. |last2=Smith |first2=Gregory T. |last3=Spillane |first3=Nichea S. |last4=Fischer |first4=Sarah |last5=Annus |first5=Agnes M. |last6=Peterson |first6=Claire |journal=Psychological Assessment |volume=19 |pages=107–18 |pmid=17371126 |issue=1}}</ref> is a revised version of the UPPS, including 59 items. It assesses an additional personality pathway to impulsive behavior, Positive Urgency, in addition to the four pathways assessed in the original version of the scale: Urgency (now Negative Urgency), (lack of) Premeditation, (lack of) Perseverance, and Sensation Seeking

UPPS-P short version (UPPS-Ps)<ref name="Billieuxa 2011">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.comppsych.2011.09.001 |title=Validation of a short French version of the UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale |year=2012 |last1=Billieux |first1=Joël |last2=Rochat |first2=Lucien |last3=Ceschi |first3=Grazia |last4=Carré |first4=Arnaud |last5=Offerlin-Meyer |first5=Isabelle |last6=Defeldre |first6=Anne-Catherine |last7=Khazaal |first7=Yasser |last8=Besche-Richard |first8=Chrystel |last9=Van Der Linden |first9=Martial |journal=Comprehensive Psychiatry |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=609–15 |pmid=22036009  }}</ref> is  20-item scale that evaluates five different impulsivity facets (4 items per dimension).

UPPS-R Interview<ref name="Smith 2007">{{cite conference |last1=Smith |first1=G. T. |last2=Fischer |first2=S. |last3=Cyders |first3=M. A. |last4=Annus |first4=A. M. |last5=Spillane |first5=N. S. |year=2007 |title=Toward a comprehensive model of multiple dispositions to rash action |conference=Annual International Meeting of the Research Society on Alcoholism |location=Chicago, IL}}</ref> is a semi-structured interview that measures the degree to which individuals exhibit the various components of impulsivity assessed by the UPPS-P.

==== Lifetime History of Impulsive Behaviors ====
Lifetime History of Impulsive Behaviors (LHIB)<ref name="Schmidt 2000">{{cite book |last=Schmidt |first=Catherine A. |year=2000 |title=Development and Validation of the Lifetime History of Impulsive Behaviors Interview and Self-Report Measures |type=PhD Thesis |oclc=48501893}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref>  is a 53-item questionnaire designed to assess lifetime history of impulsive behavior (as opposed to impulsive tendencies) as well as the level of distress and impairment associated with these behaviors.<ref name="Closkey 2009">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.jpsychires.2009.01.002 |title=Evaluation of behavioral impulsivity and aggression tasks as endophenotypes for borderline personality disorder |year=2009 |last1=McCloskey |first1=Michael S. |last2=New |first2=Antonia S. |last3=Siever |first3=Larry J. |last4=Goodman |first4=Marianne |last5=Koenigsberg |first5=Harold W. |last6=Flory |first6=Janine D. |last7=Coccaro |first7=Emil F. |journal=Journal of Psychiatric Research |volume=43 |issue=12 |pages=1036–48 |pmid=19232640 |pmc=2853811}}</ref> The assessment battery was designed to measure the following six dimensions: (a) impulsivity, (b) sensation seeking, (c) trait anxiety, (d) state depression, (e) empathy, and (f) social desirability. The LHIB consists of scales for clinically significant impulsivity, non-clinically significant impulsivity, and impulsivity related distress/impairment.<ref name="Closkey 2009" />

==== Behavioral Inhibition System/Behavioral Activation System ====
Behavioral Inhibition System/Behavioral Activation System (BIS/BAS)<ref name="Carver1994">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.67.2.319 |title=Behavioral inhibition, behavioral activation, and affective responses to impending reward and punishment: The BIS/BAS Scales |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1994-08_67_2/page/319 |year=1994 |last1=Carver |first1=Charles S. |last2=White |first2=Teri L. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=319–333 |bibcode=1994JPSP...67..319C |s2cid=6452203 }}</ref> was developed based on the [[Gray's biopsychological theory of personality]] which suggests that there are two general motivational systems that underlie behavior and affect: BIS and BAS. This 20-item self-report questionnaire is designed to assess dispositional BIS and BAS sensitivities.

==== Impulsive/Premeditated Aggression Scale ====
Impulsive/Premeditated Aggression Scale (IPAS)<ref name="Stanford 2003">{{cite journal |doi=10.1177/1073191103010002009 |title=Characterizing Aggressive Behavior |year=2003 |last1=Stanford |first1=Matthew S. |last2=Houston |first2=Rebecca J. |last3=Mathias |first3=Charles W. |last4=Villemarette-Pittman |first4=Nicole R. |last5=Helfritz |first5=Laura E. |last6=Conklin |first6=Sarah M. |journal=Assessment |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=183–90 |pmid=12801190|s2cid=28427132 }}</ref> is a 30-item self-report questionnaire. Half of the items describe impulsive [[aggression]] and half the items describe premeditated aggression. Aggressive [[behavior]] has traditionally been classified into two distinct subtypes, impulsive or premeditated. Impulsive aggression is defined as a hair-trigger aggressive response to provocation with loss of behavioral control.<ref name="Stanford 2003" /> Premeditated aggression is defined as a planned or conscious aggressive act, not spontaneous or related to an agitated state.<ref name="Stanford 2003" /> The IPAS is designed to characterize aggressive behavior as predominately impulsive or predominately premeditated in nature.<ref name="Stanford 2003" /> Those subjects who clustered on the impulsive factor showed a broad range of [[emotion]]al and [[cognition|cognitive]] impairments; those who clustered on the premeditated factor showed a greater inclination for aggression and [[anti-social behaviour]].<ref name="Stanford 2003" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mathias |first1=Charles W. |last2=Stanford |first2=Matthew S. |last3=Marsh |first3=Dawn M. |last4=Frick |first4=Paul J. |last5=Moeller |first5=F. Gerard |last6=Swann |first6=Alan C. |last7=Dougherty |first7=Donald M. |title=Characterizing aggressive behavior with the Impulsive/Premeditated Aggression Scale among adolescents with conduct disorder |journal=Psychiatry Research |date=June 2007 |volume=151 |issue=3 |pages=231–242 |doi=10.1016/j.psychres.2006.11.001 |pmid=17383014 |pmc=1994790 }}</ref>

==== Padua Inventory ====
The Padua Inventory (PI) consists of 60 items describing common obsessional and compulsive behavior and allows investigation of such problems in normal and clinical subjects.<ref>{{cite web |last=Burns |first=G.L. |year=1995 |title=Padua Inventory-Washington State University Revision |url=http://www.commondataelements.ninds.nih.gov/CRFs/Padua%20Inventory%20-%20Washington%20State%20University%20Revision.doc?CrfId=F1268 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180605032345/https://www.commondataelements.ninds.nih.gov/CRFs/Padua%20Inventory%20-%20Washington%20State%20University%20Revision.doc?CrfId=F1268 |archive-date=2018-06-05 }}</ref>

=== Behavioral paradigms ===
A wide variety of behavioral tests have been devised for the assessment of impulsivity in both clinical and experimental settings. While no single test is a perfect predictor or a sufficient replacement for an actual clinical diagnosis, when used in conjunction with parent/teacher reports, behavioral surveys, and other diagnostic criteria, the utility of behavioral paradigms lies in their ability to narrow in on specific, discrete aspects of the impulsivity umbrella. Quantifying specific deficits is of use to the clinician and the experimenter, both of whom are generally concerned with obtaining objectively measurable treatment effects.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

==== Marshmallow test ====
One widely recognizable test for impulsivity is the delay of gratification paradigm commonly known as the [[Stanford marshmallow experiment|'marshmallow test']].<ref name="Mischel1972" /> Developed in the 1960s to assess 'willpower' and self-control in preschoolers, the marshmallow test consists of placing a single marshmallow in front of a child and informing them that they will be left alone in the room for some duration. The child is told that if the marshmallow remains uneaten when the experimenter returns, they will be awarded a second marshmallow, both of which can then be eaten.{{citation needed|date=April 2019}}<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Mischel|first1=Walter|last2=Ebbesen|first2=Ebbe B.|last3=Raskoff Zeiss|first3=Antonette|date=1972|title=Cognitive and attentional mechanisms in delay of gratification.|url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1972-02_21_2/page/204|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|language=en|volume=21|issue=2|pages=204–218|doi=10.1037/h0032198|issn=1939-1315|pmid=5010404}}</ref>

Despite its simplicity and ease of administration, evidence from longitudinal studies suggests that the number of seconds preschoolers wait to obtain the second marshmallow is predictive of higher SAT scores, better social and emotional coping in adolescence, higher educational achievement, and less cocaine/crack use.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.54.4.687 |title=The nature of adolescent competencies predicted by preschool delay of gratification |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_1988-04_54_4/page/687 |year=1988 |last1=Mischel |first1=Walter |last2=Shoda |first2=Yuichi |last3=Peake |first3=Philip K. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=54 |issue=4 |pages=687–96 |pmid=3367285 |s2cid=42623330 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0012-1649.26.6.978 |title=Predicting adolescent cognitive and self-regulatory competencies from preschool delay of gratification: Identifying diagnostic conditions |year=1990 |last1=Shoda |first1=Yuichi |last2=Mischel |first2=Walter |last3=Peake |first3=Philip K. |journal=Developmental Psychology |volume=26 |issue=6 |pages=978–86 |s2cid=4259680 |url=https://scholarworks.smith.edu/psy_facpubs/79 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.79.5.776 |title=Regulating the interpersonal self: Strategic self-regulation for coping with rejection sensitivity |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-personality-and-social-psychology_2000-11_79_5/page/776 |year=2000 |last1=Ayduk |first1=Ozlem |author-link1=Ozlem Ayduk |last2=Mendoza-Denton |first2=Rodolfo |last3=Mischel |first3=Walter |last4=Downey |first4=Geraldine |last5=Peake |first5=Philip K. |last6=Rodriguez |first6=Monica |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=79 |issue=5 |pages=776–92 |pmid=11079241|s2cid=6345213 }}</ref>

==== Delay discounting ====

Like the marshmallow test, [[Hyperbolic discounting|delay discounting]] is also a delay of gratification paradigm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Mazur |first1=James E. |chapter=An adjusting procedure for studying delayed reinforcement |editor1-last=Commons |editor1-first=Michael L. |editor2-last=Mazur |editor2-first=James E. |editor3-last=Nevin |editor3-first=John Anthony |display-editors = 3 |editor4-last=Rachlin |editor4-first=Howard |series=Quantitative analyses of behavior: Vol. 5 |title=The effect of delay and of intervening events on reinforcement value |location=Hillsdale, NJ |publisher=Erlbaum |year=1987 |pages=55–73 |isbn=9781317838074 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1q5mAgAAQBAJ&pg=PA55 }}</ref> It is designed around the principle that the subjective value of a reinforcer decreases, or is 'discounted,' as the delay to reinforcement increases. Subjects are given varying choices between smaller, immediate rewards and larger, [[delayed gratification|delayed rewards]]. By manipulating reward magnitude and/or reward delay over multiple trials, 'indifference' points can be estimated whereby choosing the small, immediate reward, or the large, delayed reward are about equally likely. Subjects are labeled impulsive when their indifference points decline more steeply as a function of delay compared to the normal population (i.e. greater preference for immediate reward). Unlike the marshmallow test, delay discounting does not require verbal instruction and can be implemented on non-human animals.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Winstanley |first1=Catharine A. |last2=Dalley |first2=Jeffrey W. |last3=Theobald |first3=David E. H. |last4=Robbins |first4=Trevor W. |title=Global 5-HT depletion attenuates the ability of amphetamine to decrease impulsive choice on a delay-discounting task in rats |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychopharmacology_2003-11_170_3/page/320 |journal=Psychopharmacology |date=November 2003 |volume=170 |issue=3 |pages=320–331 |doi=10.1007/s00213-003-1546-3 |pmid=12955303 |s2cid=143605377 }}</ref>

==== Go/no-go and stop-signal reaction time tasks ====

Two common tests of response inhibition used in humans are the go/no-go task, and a slight variant known as the stop-signal reaction time (SSRT) test. During a go/no-task, the participant is trained over multiple trials to make a particular response (e.g., a key-press) when presented with a 'go' signal. On some trials, a 'stop' signal is presented just prior to, or simultaneously with the 'go' signal, and the subject must inhibit the impending response.

The SSRT test is similar, except that the 'stop' signal is presented after the 'go' signal. This small modification increases the difficulty of inhibiting the 'go' response, because the participant has typically already initiated the 'go' response by the time the 'stop' signal is presented.<ref name="Winstanley" /> The participant is instructed to respond as fast as possible to the 'go' signal while maintaining the highest possible inhibition accuracy (on no-go trials). During the task, the time at which the 'stop' signal is presented (the stop signal delay or SSD) is dynamically adjusted to match the time after the 'go' signal at which the participant is just able/unable to inhibit their 'go' response. If the participant fails to inhibit their 'go' response, the 'stop' signal is moved slightly closer to the original 'go' signal, and if the participant successfully inhibits their 'go' response, the 'stop' signal is moved slightly ahead in time. The SSRT is thus measured as the average 'go' response time minus the average 'stop' signal presentation time (SSD).

==== Balloon Analogue Risk Task ====

The balloon analogue risk task (BART) was designed to assess risk-taking behavior.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/1076-898X.8.2.75 |title=Evaluation of a behavioral measure of risk taking: The Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-experimental-psychology-applied_2002-06_8_2/page/75 |year=2002 |last1=Lejuez |first1=C. W. |last2=Read |first2=Jennifer P. |last3=Kahler |first3=Christopher W. |last4=Richards |first4=Jerry B. |last5=Ramsey |first5=Susan E. |last6=Stuart |first6=Gregory L. |last7=Strong |first7=David R. |last8=Brown |first8=Richard A. |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=75–84 |pmid=12075692 |s2cid=7523498 }}</ref> Subjects are presented with a computer depiction of a balloon that can be incrementally inflated by pressing a response key. As the balloon inflates, the subject accumulates rewards with each new key-press. The balloon is programmed with a constant probability of popping. If the balloon pops, all rewards for that balloon are lost, or the subject may choose to stop inflating and 'bank' the reward for that balloon at any time. Therefore, more key-presses equate to greater reward, but also greater probability of popping and cancelling rewards for that trial. The BART assumes that those with an affinity for 'risk-taking' are more likely to pop the balloon, earning less reward overall than the typical population.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

==== Iowa Gambling Task ====

The [[Iowa gambling task]]  (IGT) is a test originally meant to measure decision making specifically within individuals who have ventromedial prefrontal cortex damage.<ref name="Bechara" /> The concept of impulsivity as relates to the IGT is one in which impulsive decisions are a function of an individual's lack of ability to make rational decisions over time due to an over amplification of emotional/somatic  reward.<ref name="Buelow, M. T. 2009">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s11065-009-9083-4 |title=Construct Validity of the Iowa Gambling Task |year=2009 |last1=Buelow |first1=Melissa T. |last2=Suhr |first2=Julie A. |journal=Neuropsychology Review |volume=19 |pages=102–14 |pmid=19194801 |issue=1 |s2cid=8248057 }}</ref> In the IGT individuals are provided four decks of cards to choose from.  Two of these decks provide much higher rewards but the deductions are also much higher while the second two decks have lower rewards per card but also much lower deductions.  Over time anyone who chooses predominantly from the high rewards decks will lose money while those who choose from the smaller rewards decks will gain money.

The IGT uses hot and cold processes in its concept of decision making.<ref name="Buelow, M. T. 2009"/>  Hot decision making involves emotional responses to the material presented based on motivation related to reward and punishment.  Cold processes occur when an individual uses rational cognitive determinations when making decisions.  Combined an individual should gain a positive emotional reaction when choices have beneficial consequences and will have [[negative emotion]]al responses tied to choices that have greater negative consequences.  In general, healthy responders to the IGT will begin to drift to the lower gain decks as they realize that they are gaining more money than they lose both through an ability to recognize that one is more consistently providing rewards as well as through the emotions related to winning consistently.  However, those who have emotional deficits will fail to recognize that they are losing money over time and will continue to be more influenced by the exhilaration of higher value rewards without being influenced by the negative emotions of the loses associated with them.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

For more information concerning these process refer to the '''[[Somatic marker hypothesis]]'''

==== Differential Reinforcement of Low Response Rate Task ====

Differential reinforcement of low response rate (DRL) described by Ferster and Skinner<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ferster |first1=Charles B. |last2=Skinner |first2=B. F. |title=Schedules of reinforcement |location=New York |year=1957|publisher=Appleton-Century-Crofts|lccn=57006418|oclc=4596140}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> is used to encourage low rates of responding. It is derived from research in [[operant conditioning]] that provides an excellent opportunity to measure the hyperactive child's ability to inhibit behavioral responding. Hyperactive children were relatively unable to perform efficiently on the task, and this deficit endured regardless of age, [[IQ]], or experimental condition.<ref name="Gordon, M. 1979">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/BF00916541 |title=The assessment of impulsivity and mediating behaviors in hyperactive and nonhyperactive boys |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-abnormal-child-psychology_1979-09_7_3/page/317 |year=1979 |last1=Gordon |first1=Michael |journal=Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=317–26 |pmid=489853 |s2cid=31953219 }}</ref> Therefore, it can be used to discriminate accurately between teacher rated and parent rated hyperactive and nonhyperactive children. In this procedure, responses that occur before a set time interval has passed are not reinforced and reset the time required between behaviors.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

In a study, a child was taken to the experimental room and told that they were going to play a game in which they had a chance to win a lot of M&M's.  Every time they made the light of the reward indicator by pressing a red button, they would earn an M&M's. However, they had to wait a while (6 seconds) before they could press it to get another point. If they had pressed the button too soon, then they would have not gotten a point, and the light would not go on, and they had to wait a while before they could press it to get another point.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

Researchers have also observed that subjects in a time-based situation will often engage in a sequence or chain of behaviors between reinforceable responses.<ref name="Gordon, M. 1979"/> This is because this collateral behavior sequence helps the subject "wait out" the required [[Time|temporal]] delay between responses.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

==== Other ====
Other common impulsivity tasks include the [[Continuous Performance Task|Continuous performance task]] (CPT), [[5-Choice Serial Reaction Time Task|5-choice serial reaction time task]] (5-CSRTT), [[stroop effect|Stroop task]], and Matching Familiar Figures Task.

== Pharmacology and neurobiology ==

=== Neurobiological findings ===
Although the precise neural mechanisms underlying disorders of impulse control are not fully known, the prefrontal cortex (PFC) is the brain region most ubiquitously implicated in impulsivity.<ref name="Castellanos">{{cite journal |pmid=12154363 |year=2002 |last1=Castellanos |first1=F. Xavier |last2=Tannock |first2=Rosemary |title=Neuroscience of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: The search for endophenotypes |volume=3 |issue=8 |pages=617–28 |doi=10.1038/nrn896 |journal=Nature Reviews Neuroscience |s2cid=3346437 }}</ref>  Damage to the prefrontal cortex has been associated with difficulties preparing to act, switching between response alternatives, and inhibiting inappropriate responses.<ref name="Winstanley">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.cpr.2006.01.001 |title=Behavioral models of impulsivity in relation to ADHD: Translation between clinical and preclinical studies |year=2006 |last1=Winstanley |first1=Catharine A. |last2=Eagle |first2=Dawn M. |last3=Robbins |first3=Trevor W. |journal=Clinical Psychology Review |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=379–95 |pmid=16504359 |pmc=1892795}}</ref> Recent research has uncovered additional regions of interest, as well as highlighted particular subregions of the PFC, that can be tied to performance in specific behavioral tasks.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}}

==== Delay discounting ====

Excitotoxic lesions in the nucleus accumbens core have been shown to increase preference for the smaller, immediate reward, whereas lesions to the nucleus accumbens shell have had no observable effect. Additionally, lesions of the basolateral amygdala, a region tied closely to the PFC, negatively affect impulsive choice similarly to what is observed in the nucleus accumbens core lesions.<ref name="dalley">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2011.01.020 |title=Impulsivity, Compulsivity, and Top-Down Cognitive Control |year=2011 |last1=Dalley |first1=Jeffrey W. |last2=Everitt |first2=Barry J. |last3=Robbins |first3=Trevor W. |journal=Neuron |volume=69 |issue=4 |pages=680–94 |pmid=21338879|s2cid=7952439 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Moreover, dorsal striatum may also be involved in impulsive choice in an intricate manner.<ref name="Kim">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/nyas.13961 |title=The role of the dorsal striatum in choice impulsivity |year=2018 |last1=Kim |first1=BaekSun |last2=Im |first2=Heh-In |journal=Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences |pmid=30277562 |volume=1451 |issue=1 |pages=92–111|s2cid=52897511 }}</ref>

==== Go/No-go and Stop-signal reaction time test ====

The [[orbitofrontal cortex]] is now thought to play a role in disinhibiting,<ref name="Berlin">{{cite journal |doi=10.1093/brain/awh135 |title=Impulsivity, time perception, emotion and reinforcement sensitivity in patients with orbitofrontal cortex lesions |year=2004 |last1=Berlin |first1=H. A. |journal=Brain |volume=127 |issue=5 |pages=1108–26 |pmid=14985269 |last2=Rolls |first2=ET |last3=Kischka |first3=U|doi-access=free }}</ref> and injury to other brain structures, such as to the right inferior frontal gyrus, a specific subregion of the PFC, has been associated with deficits in stop-signal inhibition.<ref name="Aron2003">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nn1003 |title=Stop-signal inhibition disrupted by damage to right inferior frontal gyrus in humans |year=2003 |last1=Aron |first1=Adam R. |last2=Fletcher |first2=Paul C. |last3=Bullmore |first3=Ed T. |last4=Sahakian |first4=Barbara J. |last5=Robbins |first5=Trevor W. |journal=Nature Neuroscience |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=115–6 |pmid=12536210 |s2cid=10096947 }}</ref>

==== 5-Choice Serial Reaction Time Task (5-CSRTT) and Differential Reinforcement of Low rates (DRL) ====
As with delay discounting, lesion studies have implicated the core region of the nucleus accumbens in response inhibition for both DRL and 5-CSRTT.  Premature responses in the 5-CSRTT may also be modulated by other systems within the ventral striatum.{{citation needed|date=June 2013}} In the 5-CSRTT, lesions of the anterior cingulate cortex have been shown to increase impulsive responding, and lesions to the prelimbic cortex impair attentional performance.<ref name="Muir">{{cite journal |doi=10.1093/cercor/6.3.470 |title=The Cerebral Cortex of the Rat and Visual Attentional Function: Dissociable Effects of Mediofrontal, Cingulate, Anterior Dorsolateral, and Parietal Cortex Lesions on a Five-Choice Serial Reaction Time Task |year=1996 |last1=Muir |first1=Janice L. |last2=Everitt |first2=Barry J. |last3=Robbins |first3=Trevor W. |journal=Cerebral Cortex |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=470–81 |pmid=8670672|doi-access=free }}</ref>

==== Iowa Gambling Task ====
Patients with damage to the ventromedial frontal cortex exhibit poor decision-making and persist in making risky choices in the [[Iowa gambling task|Iowa Gambling Task]].<ref name="Bechara">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/0010-0277(94)90018-3 |title=Insensitivity to future consequences following damage to human prefrontal cortex |year=1994 |last1=Bechara |first1=Antoine |last2=Damasio |first2=Antonio R. |last3=Damasio |first3=Hanna |last4=Anderson |first4=Steven W. |journal=Cognition |volume=50 |pages=7–15 |pmid=8039375 |issue=1–3 |s2cid=204981454 }}</ref><ref name="Bechara2">{{cite journal |first1=Antoine |last1=Bechara |first2=Hanna |last2=Damasio |first3=Antonio R. |last3=Damasio |first4=Gregory P. |last4=Lee |title=Different Contributions of the Human Amygdala and Ventromedial Prefrontal Cortex to Decision-Making |journal=The Journal of Neuroscience |pmid=10377356 |pmc=6782338 |year=1999 |volume=19 |issue=13 |pages=5473–81|doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.19-13-05473.1999 }}</ref>

=== Neurochemical and pharmacological findings ===

The primary pharmacological treatments for ADHD are methylphenidate (Ritalin) and amphetamine. Both methylphenidate and amphetamines block re-uptake of dopamine and norepinephrine into the pre-synaptic neuron, acting to increase post-synaptic levels of dopamine and norepinephrine. Of these two monoamines, increased availability of dopamine is considered the primary cause for the ameliorative effects of ADHD medications, whereas increased levels of norepinephrine may be efficacious only to the extent that it has downstream, indirect effects on dopamine.<ref name="Wilens2006">{{cite journal |first1=Timothy E. |last1=Wilens |title=Mechanism of Action of Agents Used in Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry |pmid=16961428 |url=http://article.psychiatrist.com/?ContentType=START&ID=10002653 |year=2006 |volume=67 |issue=Suppl 8 |pages=32–8 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130628023108/http://article.psychiatrist.com/?ContentType=START&ID=10002653 |archive-date=2013-06-28 }}</ref>  
The effectiveness of dopamine re-uptake inhibitors in treating the symptoms of ADHD has led to the hypothesis that ADHD may arise from low tonic levels of dopamine (particularly in the fronto-limbic circuitry), but evidence in support of this theory is mixed.<ref name="Dougherty">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0140-6736(99)04030-1 |title=Dopamine transporter density in patients with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder |year=1999 |last1=Dougherty |first1=Darin D |last2=Bonab |first2=Ali A |last3=Spencer |first3=Thomas J |last4=Rauch |first4=Scott L |last5=Madras |first5=Bertha K |last6=Fischman |first6=Alan J |journal=The Lancet |volume=354 |issue=9196 |pages=2132–3 |pmid=10609822 |s2cid=7420578 }}</ref><ref name="Volkow">{{cite journal |doi=10.1001/jama.2009.1308 |title=Evaluating Dopamine Reward Pathway in ADHD: Clinical Implications |year=2009 |last1=Volkow |first1=Nora D. |journal=JAMA |volume=302 |issue=10 |pages=1084–91 |pmid=19738093 |last2=Wang |first2=GJ |last3=Kollins |first3=SH |last4=Wigal |first4=TL |last5=Newcorn |first5=JH |last6=Telang |first6=F |last7=Fowler |first7=JS |last8=Zhu |first8=W |last9=Logan |first9=J |last10=Ma |first10=Yeming |last11=Pradhan |first11=Kith |last12=Wong |first12=Christopher |last13=Swanson |first13=James M. |pmc=2958516 |display-authors=8 }}</ref>

== Genetics ==

There are several difficulties when it comes to trying to identify a [[gene]] for [[complex traits]] such as impulsivity, such as [[genetic heterogeneity]].  Another difficulty is that the genes in question might sometimes show incomplete penetrance, "where a given gene variant does not always cause the phenotype".<ref name="Wallis">{{cite journal |doi=10.1093/jpepsy/jsn049 |title=Review: Genetics of Attention Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder |year=2008 |last1=Wallis |first1=D. |last2=Russell |first2=H. F. |last3=Muenke |first3=M. |journal=Journal of Pediatric Psychology |volume=33 |issue=10 |pages=1085–99 |pmid=18522996|doi-access=free }}</ref>  Much of the research on the genetics of impulsivity-related disorders, such as ADHD, is based on family or [[Genetic linkage|linkage studies]].<ref name="Kempf">{{cite book |last1=Kempf |first1=Lucas |last2=Weinberger |first2=Daniel R |year=2009 |chapter=Molecular Genetics and Bioinformatics: An Outline for Neuropsychological Genetics |editor1-first=Terry E |editor1-last=Goldberg |editor2-first=Daniel R |editor2-last=Weinberger |title=The Genetics of Cognitive Neuroscience |url=https://archive.org/details/geneticscognitiv00gold |pages=[https://archive.org/details/geneticscognitiv00gold/page/n15 3]–26 |publisher=MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-01307-9}}</ref> There are several genes of interest that have been studied in an attempt to find the major genetic contributors to impulsivity.  Some of these genes are:
* [[DAT1]]  is the dopamine transporter gene which is responsible for the active reuptake of dopamine from the neural synapse. DAT1 [[polymorphism (biology)|polymorphism]]s have been shown to be linked to hyperactivity and ADHD.<ref name="Brookes">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/sj.mp.4001869 |title=The analysis of 51 genes in DSM-IV combined type attention deficit hyperactivity disorder: Association signals in DRD4, DAT1 and 16 other genes |year=2006 |last1=Brookes |first1=K |last2=Xu |first2=X |last3=Chen |first3=W |last4=Zhou |first4=K |last5=Neale |first5=B |last6=Lowe |first6=N |last7=Aneey |first7=R |last8=Franke |first8=B |last9=Gill |first9=M |last10=Ebstein |first10=R |last11=Buitelaar |first11=J |last12=Sham |first12=P |last13=Campbell |first13=D |last14=Knight |first14=J |last15=Andreou |first15=P |last16=Altink |first16=M |last17=Arnold |first17=R |last18=Boer |first18=F |last19=Buschgens |first19=C |last20=Butler |first20=L |last21=Christiansen |first21=H |last22=Feldman |first22=L |last23=Fleischman |first23=K |last24=Fliers |first24=E |last25=Howe-Forbes |first25=R |last26=Goldfarb |first26=A |last27=Heise |first27=A |last28=Gabriëls |first28=I |last29=Korn-Lubetzki |first29=I |last30=Marco |first30=R |journal=Molecular Psychiatry |volume=11 |issue=10 |pages=934–53 |pmid=16894395|s2cid=6266643 |display-authors=8 |doi-access=free |hdl=1871/17985 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>  
* [[DRD4]] is the dopamine D4 receptor gene and is associated with ADHD and novelty seeking behaviors.<ref name="Wallis" /><ref name="Ptacek">{{cite journal |doi=10.12659/MSM.881925 |title=Dopamine D4 receptor gene DRD4 and its association with psychiatric disorders |year=2011 |last1=Ptacek |first1=Radek |last2=Kuzelova |first2=Hana |last3=Stefano |first3=George B. |journal=Medical Science Monitor |volume=17 |issue=9 |pages=RA215–20 |pmid=21873960 |pmc=3560519}}</ref> It has been proposed that novelty seeking is associated with impulsivity.  Mice deficient for DRD4 have shown less behavioral responses to novelty.<ref name="Dulawa">{{cite journal |first1=Stephanie C. |last1=Dulawa |first2=David K. |last2=Grandy |first3=Malcolm J. |last3=Low |first4=Martin P. |last4=Paulus |first5=Mark A. |last5=Geye |title=Dopamine D4 Receptor-Knock-Out Mice Exhibit Reduced Exploration of Novel Stimuli |journal=The Journal of Neuroscience |pmid=10531457 |pmc=6782928 |year=1999 |volume=19 |issue=21 |pages=9550–6|doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.19-21-09550.1999 }}</ref>
* [[5HT2A]] is the serotonin receptor gene.  The serotonin 2A receptor gene has been associated with both hyper locomotion, ADHD, as well as impulsivity.  Subjects with a particular polymorphism of the 5HT2A gene made more commission errors during a punishment-reward condition in a go/no-go task.<ref name="Nomura">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s00213-006-0398-z |title=Involvement of a polymorphism in the 5-HT2A receptor gene in impulsive behavior |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_psychopharmacology_2006-07_187_1/page/30 |year=2006 |last1=Nomura |first1=Michio |last2=Kusumi |first2=Ichiro |last3=Kaneko |first3=Masayuki |last4=Masui |first4=Takuya |last5=Daiguji |first5=Makoto |last6=Ueno |first6=Takeji |last7=Koyama |first7=Tsukasa |last8=Nomura |first8=Yasuyuki |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=187 |pages=30–5 |pmid=16767413 |issue=1 |s2cid=12155339 }}</ref>
* [[HTR2B]] a serotonin receptor gene.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nature09629 |title=A population-specific HTR2B stop codon predisposes to severe impulsivity |year=2010 |last1=Bevilacqua |first1=Laura |last2=Doly |first2=Stéphane |last3=Kaprio |first3=Jaakko |last4=Yuan |first4=Qiaoping |last5=Tikkanen |first5=Roope |last6=Paunio |first6=Tiina |last7=Zhou |first7=Zhifeng |last8=Wedenoja |first8=Juho |last9=Maroteaux |first9=Luc |last10=Diaz |first10=Silvina |last11=Belmer |first11=Arnaud |last12=Hodgkinson |first12=Colin A. |last13=Dell'Osso |first13=Liliana |last14=Suvisaari |first14=Jaana |last15=Coccaro |first15=Emil |last16=Rose |first16=Richard J. |last17=Peltonen |first17=Leena |last18=Virkkunen |first18=Matti |last19=Goldman |first19=David |journal=Nature |volume=468 |issue=7327 |pages=1061–6 |pmid=21179162 |pmc=3183507|display-authors=8 |bibcode=2010Natur.468.1061B }}</ref>
* [[CTNNA2]] encodes for a brain-expressed α-catenin that has been associated with Excitement-Seeking in a [[genome-wide association study]] (GWAS) of 7860 individuals.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/tp.2011.42 |title=Meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies identifies common variants in CTNNA2 associated with excitement-seeking |year=2011 |last1=Terracciano |first1=A |last2=Esko |first2=T |last3=Sutin |first3=A R |last4=De Moor |first4=M H M |last5=Meirelles |first5=O |last6=Zhu |first6=G |last7=Tanaka |first7=T |last8=Giegling |first8=I |last9=Nutile |first9=T |last10=Realo |first10=A |last11=Allik |first11=J |last12=Hansell |first12=N K |last13=Wright |first13=M J |last14=Montgomery |first14=G W |last15=Willemsen |first15=G |last16=Hottenga |first16=J-J |last17=Friedl |first17=M |last18=Ruggiero |first18=D |last19=Sorice |first19=R |last20=Sanna |first20=S |last21=Cannas |first21=A |last22=Räikkönen |first22=K |last23=Widen |first23=E |last24=Palotie |first24=A |last25=Eriksson |first25=J G |last26=Cucca |first26=F |last27=Krueger |first27=R F |last28=Lahti |first28=J |last29=Luciano |first29=M |last30=Smoller |first30=J W |journal=Translational Psychiatry |volume=1 |issue=10 |pages=e49– |pmid=22833195 |pmc=3309493|display-authors=8 }}</ref>

== Intervention ==

=== Interventions to impact impulsivity generally ===

While impulsivity can take on pathological forms (e.g. substance use disorder, ADHD), there are less severe, non-clinical forms of problematic impulsivity in many people's daily lives.  Research on the different facets of impulsivity can inform small interventions to change decision making and reduce impulsive behavior<ref name="Ratner et al 2008">{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s11002-008-9044-3 |title=How behavioral decision research can enhance consumer welfare: From freedom of choice to paternalistic intervention |year=2008 |last1=Ratner |first1=Rebecca K. |last2=Soman |first2=Dilip |last3=Zauberman |first3=Gal |last4=Ariely |first4=Dan |last5=Carmon |first5=Ziv |last6=Keller |first6=Punam A. |last7=Kim |first7=B. Kyu |last8=Lin |first8=Fern |last9=Malkoc |first9=Selin |last10=Small |first10=Deborah A. |last11=Wertenbroch |first11=Klaus |journal=Marketing Letters |volume=19 |issue=3–4 |pages=383–397|s2cid=5880506 |display-authors=8 }}</ref> For example, changing cognitive representations of rewards (e.g. making long term rewards seem more concrete) and/or creating situations of "[[precommitment]]" (eliminating the option of changing one's mind later)  can reduce the preference for immediate reward seen in delay discounting.<ref name=" Ratner et al 2008" />

==== Brain training ====
Brain training interventions include laboratory-based interventions (e.g. training using tasks like go/no go) as well as community, family, and school based interventions that are ecologically valid (e.g. teaching techniques for regulating emotions or behaviors) and can be used with individuals with non-clinical levels of impulsivity.<ref name="Berkman et al 2012">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1750-8606.2012.00248.x |title=Training Self-Control: A Domain-General Translational Neuroscience Approach |year=2012 |last1=Berkman |first1=Elliot T. |last2=Graham |first2=Alice M. |last3=Fisher |first3=Philip A. |journal=Child Development Perspectives |pages=374–384|pmc=3722070 |pmid=23894251 |volume=6|issue=4 }}</ref>  Both sorts of interventions are aimed at improving executive functioning and self-control capacities, with different interventions specifically targeting different aspects of executive functioning like inhibitory control, working memory, or attention.<ref name=" Berkman et al 2012" /> Emerging evidence suggests that brain training interventions may succeed in impacting executive function, including inhibitory control.<ref name="Hoffman et al 2012">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.tics.2012.01.006 |title=Executive functions and self-regulation |year=2012 |last1=Hofmann |first1=Wilhelm |last2=Schmeichel |first2=Brandon J. |last3=Baddeley |first3=Alan D. |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=174–80 |pmid=22336729 |s2cid=13309571 }}</ref> Inhibitory control training specifically is accumulating evidence that it can help individuals resist temptation to consume high calorie food<ref name="Houben Jansen 2011">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.appet.2010.12.017 |title=Training inhibitory control. A recipe for resisting sweet temptations |year=2011 |last1=Houben |first1=Katrijn |last2=Jansen |first2=Anita |journal=Appetite |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=345–9 |pmid=21185896|s2cid=10664026 |url=https://zenodo.org/record/894400 }}</ref> and drinking behavior.<ref name="Houben Nederkorn 2011">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2010.12.011 |title=Resisting temptation: Decreasing alcohol-related affect and drinking behavior by training response inhibition |year=2011 |last1=Houben |first1=Katrijn |last2=Nederkoorn |first2=Chantal |last3=Wiers |first3=Reinout W. |last4=Jansen |first4=Anita |journal=Drug and Alcohol Dependence |volume=116 |pages=132–6 |pmid=21288663 |issue=1–3|url=https://cris.maastrichtuniversity.nl/ws/files/73261520/Jansen_2011_Resisting_temptation_decreasing_alcohol_related.pdf }}</ref> Some have voiced concerns that  the favorable results of studies testing working memory training should be interpreted with caution, claiming that conclusions regarding changes to abilities are measured using single tasks, inconsistent use of working memory tasks, no-contact control groups, and subjective measurements of change.<ref name="Shipstead et al 2012">{{cite journal |doi= 10.1037/a0027473 |title= Is working memory training effective? |url= https://archive.org/details/sim_psychological-bulletin_2012-07_138_4/page/628 |year= 2012 |last1= Shipstead |first1= Zach |last2= Redick |first2= Thomas S. |last3= Engle |first3= Randall W. |journal= Psychological Bulletin |volume= 138 |issue= 4 |pages= 628–54 |pmid=22409508 |s2cid=14152894 }}</ref>

=== Treatment of specific disorders of impulsivity ===
Behavioral, psychosocial, and psychopharmacological treatments for disorders involving impulsivity are common.

==== Psychopharmacological intervention ====
Psychopharmacological intervention in disorders of impulsivity has shown evidence of positive effects; common pharmacological interventions include the use of stimulant medication, [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor]]s (SSRIs) and other [[antidepressant]]s.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/> ADHD has a well-established evidence base supporting the use of [[stimulant]] medication for the reduction of ADHD symptoms.<ref name="Swanson et al 2001">{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/00004583-200102000-00011 |pmid=11211365 |title=Clinical Relevance of the Primary Findings of the MTA: Success Rates Based on Severity of ADHD and ODD Symptoms at the End of Treatment |year=2001 |last1=Swanson |first1=James M. |last2=Kraemer |first2=Helena C. |last3=Hinshaw |first3=Stephen P. |last4=Arnold |first4=L. Eugene |last5=Conners |first5=C. Keith |last6=Abikoff |first6=Howard B. |last7=Clevenger |first7=Walter |last8=Davies |first8=Mark |last9=Elliott |first9=Glen R. |last10=Greenhill |first10=Laurence L. |last11=Hechtman |first11=Lily |last12=Hoza |first12=Betsy |last13=Jensen |first13=Peter S. |last14=March |first14=John S. |last15=Newcorn |first15=Jeffrey H. |last16=Owens |first16=Elizabeth B. |last17=Pelham |first17=William E. |last18=Schiller |first18=Ellen |last19=Severe |first19=Joanne B. |last20=Simpson |first20=Steve |last21=Vitiello |first21=Benedetto |last22=Wells |first22=Karen |last23=Wigal |first23=Timothy |last24=Wu |first24=MIN |journal=Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry |volume=40 |issue=2 |pages=168–79|s2cid=4638728 |display-authors=8 }}</ref> Pathological gambling has also been studied in drug trials, and there is evidence that gambling is responsive to [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor|SSRI]]s and other [[antidepressant]]s.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/>  Evidence based pharmacological treatment for trichotillomania is not yet available, with mixed results of studies investigating the use of SSRIs, though [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] has shown positive effects.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/> Intermittent explosive disorder is most often treated with mood stabilizers, SSRIs, [[beta blocker]]s, alpha agonists, and [[anti-psychotic]]s (all of which have shown positive effects).<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/> There is evidence that some pharmacological interventions are efficacious in treating substance-use disorders, though their use can depend on the type of substance that is abused.<ref name="McGovern Carroll 2003">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0193-953X(03)00073-X |title=Evidence-based practices for substance use disorders |year=2003 |last1=McGovern |first1=Mark P |last2=Carroll |first2=Kathleen M |journal=Psychiatric Clinics of North America |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=991–1010 |pmid=14711132 |pmc=3678283}}</ref>  Pharmacological treatments for substance-use disorders include  [[acamprosate]], [[buprenorphine]], [[disulfiram]], [[LAAM]], [[methadone]], and [[naltrexone]].<ref name=" McGovern Carroll 2003"/>

==== Behavioral interventions ====
Behavioral interventions also have a fairly strong evidence base in impulse-control disorders.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/> In ADHD, the behavioral interventions of [[Parent Management Training|behavioral parent training]], behavioral classroom management, and intensive peer-focused behavioral interventions in recreational settings meet stringent guidelines qualifying them for [[Evidence-based practice|evidence based treatment]] status.<ref name="Pelham Fabiano 2008">{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/15374410701818681 |pmid=18444058 |title=Evidence-Based Psychosocial Treatments for Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder |year=2008 |last1=Pelham |first1=William E. |last2=Fabiano |first2=Gregory A. |journal=Journal of Clinical Child & Adolescent Psychology |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=184–214|s2cid=829396 }}</ref> In addition, a recent meta-analysis of evidence-based ADHD treatment found organization training to be a well-established treatment method.<ref name=Evans2014>{{cite journal|last1=Evans|first1=SW|last2=Owens|first2=JS|last3=Bunford|first3=N|title=Evidence-based psychosocial treatments for children and adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.|journal=Journal of Clinical Child and Adolescent Psychology|date=2014|volume=43|issue=4|pages=527–51|pmid=24245813|doi=10.1080/15374416.2013.850700|pmc=4025987}}</ref> Empirically validated behavioral treatments for substance use disorder are fairly similar across substance use disorders, and include [[Integrative behavioral couples therapy|behavioral couples therapy]], [[Cognitive behavioral therapy|CBT]], [[contingency management]], [[motivational interviewing|motivational enhancement therapy]], and [[relapse prevention]].<ref name=" McGovern Carroll 2003"/>  Pyromania and kleptomania are understudied (due in large part to the illegality of the behaviors), though there is some evidence that psychotherapeutic interventions (CBT, short term counseling, day treatment programs) are efficacious in treating pyromania, while kleptomania seems to be best addressed using SSRIs.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/>  Additionally, therapies including CBT, family therapy, and [[social skills|social skill]] training have shown positive effects on explosive aggressive behaviors.<ref name="Dell'Osso 2006"/>
